(c) Take rows {1,2} and the three column-pairs {1,2},{1,3},{2,3}. Multiply the three resulting equalities. Each of the six signs sign(R_{1*}),sign(R_{2*}) appears exactly twice, so the left side is +1, while the right side is (-1)^3 = -1. Contradiction. Hence t >= 2/3. QED.

Thus rho * (2/3) <= rho * max|R_{kj}| <= 1/2, giving rho <= 3/4 and edge = rho*sqrt2 <= 3sqrt2/4.

CONSTRUCTION attaining 3sqrt2/4. Take the orthonormal frame given by the columns of (1/3)*[[2,-1,2],[2,2,-1],[-1,2,2]] (one checks each column has norm 1 and pairwise dot products 0). Put the octahedron at the center of K with circumradius rho = 3/4, i.e. vertices +- (3/4) r_k. Explicitly the three positive vertices are (1/2,1/2,-1/4), (-1/4,1/2,1/2), (1/2,-1/4,1/2) (and their negatives). All coordinates lie in [-1/2,1/2], so the octahedron lies in K; and every entry of the frame matrix equals 2/3 or 1/3 in absolute value, so max|R_{kj}| = 2/3, making the bound tight. The common edge length is sqrt((3/4)^2+(3/4)^2) = 3sqrt2/4 (verified for all 12 edges). Hence the maximum is 3sqrt2/4.

=================================================================
PART (2): largest cube inside a regular octahedron of edge 1.
=================================================================
Place the octahedron as O = { (x,y,z) : |x|+|y|+|z| <= c }, c = 1/sqrt2; its vertices are (+-1/sqrt2,0,0),(0,+-1/sqrt2,0),(0,0,+-1/sqrt2) and its edge length is sqrt((1/sqrt2)^2+(1/sqrt2)^2) = 1. A cube of edge s with center m and orthonormal edge-directions r_1,r_2,r_3 has 8 vertices m + (s/2)*d_sigma, where d_sigma = sigma_1 r_1 + sigma_2 r_2 + sigma_3 r_3, sigma in {+-1}^3. Since both O and the cube are convex, the cube lies in O iff all 8 vertices do, i.e. ||m + (s/2) d_sigma||_1 <= c for all sigma (here ||.||_1 is |x|+|y|+|z|).

UPPER BOUND. For each sigma, pairing sigma with -sigma and using ||u+v||_1 + ||u-v||_1 >= ||2v||_1:
  2c >= ||m + (s/2)d_sigma||_1 + ||m - (s/2)d_sigma||_1 >= ||s*d_sigma||_1 = s*||d_sigma||_1.
Hence s * ||d_sigma||_1 <= 2c for EVERY sigma, so s * max_sigma ||d_sigma||_1 <= 2c (center m drops out). It therefore suffices to bound max_sigma||d_sigma||_1 below.

LEMMA B. For any orthonormal r_1,r_2,r_3 in R^3, max_{sigma in {+-1}^3} ||sigma_1 r_1 + sigma_2 r_2 + sigma_3 r_3||_1 >= 1 + sqrt2.
Given Lemma B, s <= 2c/(1+sqrt2) = (2/sqrt2)/(1+sqrt2) = sqrt2/(1+sqrt2) = sqrt2(sqrt2-1) = 2 - sqrt2.

Progress toward Lemma B (the one remaining elementary step). Put a=r_1,b=r_2,c'=r_3 and let p=a+b, q=a-b. Then {p/sqrt2,q/sqrt2,c'} is orthonormal, and the four diagonal vectors are p+-c' and q+-c'. Using ||u+v||_1+||u-v||_1 = 2*sum_i max(|u_i|,|v_i|),
  max(||p+c'||_1,||p-c'||_1) >= sum_i max(|p_i|,|c'_i|),  and similarly with q.
So T := max_sigma||d_sigma||_1 >= max( sum_i max(|(a+b)_i|,|c'_i|), sum_i max(|(a-b)_i|,|c'_i|) ), and (by symmetry) the analogous bounds for the other two ways of singling out one r_k. A numerical minimization over O(3) confirms that the maximum of these expressions over the three choices is >= 1+sqrt2 for every orthonormal frame, with equality exactly at the optimal configuration below; closing this final inequality in closed form completes the upper bound. (This is the only step I have not reduced to a one-line elementary argument; everything else in Part (2) is rigorous.)

CONSTRUCTION attaining 2 - sqrt2 (this CORRECTS the reference, whose axis-parallel cube is wrong). Take the cube centered at the origin, rotated 45 degrees about the z-axis: edge-directions r_1=(1/sqrt2,1/sqrt2,0), r_2=(-1/sqrt2,1/sqrt2,0), r_3=(0,0,1), and edge s = 2 - sqrt2. Its 8 vertices are (+-s/sqrt2, 0, +-s/2) and (0, +-s/sqrt2, +-s/2). Each has ||.||_1 = s/sqrt2 + s/2 = s*(sqrt2+1)/2 = (2-sqrt2)(sqrt2+1)/2 = sqrt2/2 = 1/sqrt2 = c. Thus all 8 vertices lie exactly on the boundary of O, so the cube lies in O. (For comparison, an axis-parallel centered cube of side s has a vertex at (s/2,s/2,s/2) with ||.||_1 = 3s/2 <= c, forcing s <= 2c/3 = sqrt2/3 ~ 0.471, strictly smaller -- which is why the reference's axis-parallel claim is false.) Hence the maximum cube edge is 2 - sqrt2.

SUMMARY: (1) 3sqrt2/4 ; (2) 2 - sqrt2. Both with explicit extremal configurations; Part (1) fully proved, Part (2) construction fully proved and the upper bound reduced to the tight Lemma B (verified, last elementary step pending).
_original_solutions:
**Part (1):** We find the maximum edge length of a regular octahedron inside a unit cube.

By symmetry, we may assume the octahedron and cube share the same center. Consider the cube oriented with vertices at $(\pm 1/2, \pm 1/2, \pm 1/2)$. The circumsphere of the octahedron divides the cube into 8 sections (one per vertex). We can show that no two points in regions corresponding to adjacent octahedron-vertex assignments are far enough apart to exceed $\frac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}$, so the 6 vertices of the octahedron cannot be placed with a larger edge length.

The maximum is achieved when the octahedron has its 6 vertices at points that are optimally distributed within the cube faces. The maximum edge length is $\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}$.

**Part (2):** We find the maximum edge length of a cube inside a regular octahedron of edge length 1.

By symmetry, we may assume the cube and octahedron share the same center. The circumsphere of the cube divides the octahedron into 6 sections (one per face-center region). One can show that no two points in the same region are far enough apart to give a cube edge length exceeding $2 - \sqrt{2}$.

For a concrete construction: the regular octahedron of edge length 1 has vertices at $(\pm 1/\sqrt{2}, 0, 0)$, $(0, \pm 1/\sqrt{2}, 0)$, $(0, 0, \pm 1/\sqrt{2})$ (with inradius $1/\sqrt{6}$ and midradius $1/2$). A cube of edge length $s = 2 - \sqrt{2}$ centered at the origin with sides parallel to the coordinate axes fits inside this octahedron.

The maximum edge lengths are $\dfrac{3\sqrt{2}}{4}$ for part (1) and $2 - \sqrt{2}$ for part (2).

_audit.gap_note: Both final answers are CORRECT (3sqrt2/4 and 2-sqrt2; confirmed numerically and by explicit constructions). But the reference 'solution' contains essentially NO proof: both upper bounds are asserted with 'we can show'/'one can show' and zero content, the Part (1) construction is never given, and the Part (2) construction is stated WRONG -- it claims an AXIS-PARALLEL cube of edge 2-sqrt2 fits in the octahedron, which is FALSE (an axis-parallel centered cube only fits up to edge sqrt2/3 ~ 0.471 < 0.586; the correct optimal cube is rotated 45 degrees about a 4-fold axis). My fix: Part (1) is made fully rigorous (explicit construction + a clean upper-bound proof via the lemma 'every 3x3 orthogonal matrix has an entry of absolute value >= 2/3', proved by a cofactor/sign argument). Part (2): I give the correct construction (45-degree-rotated cube, all 8 vertices verified on the boundary) and a rigorous reduction of the upper bound to Lemma B: for orthonormal r1,r2,r3, max over signs of ||sigma1 r1+sigma2 r2+sigma3 r3||_1 >= 1+sqrt2. Lemma B is verified numerically (min = 1+sqrt2, tight) and I reduce it to the inequality T >= sum_i max(|(a+b)_i|,|c_i|), which is tight; the very last elementary step closing Lemma B's constant is the one remaining gap in the Part (2) upper bound.

problem:
Find all functions $f: \mathbb{N}_+ \to \mathbb{N}_+$ such that for any positive integers $x$ and $y$,
$$f(x) + f(y) + xy \mid xf(x) - yf(y).$$
reference_answer: $f(n) = n^2 + kn$ for some non-negative integer $k$ (equivalently, $f(n) = n(n+k)$ where $k = f(1) - 1 \in \mathbb{N}_+ \cup \{0\}$)
solutions:
**Answer:** $f(n) = n(n+k)$ for some non-negative integer $k$.

**Claim 1.** $xf(x)$ is injective.

*Proof.* Suppose $af(a) = bf(b) = C$ for some $a > b$. From $P(x,a)$ and $P(x,b)$:
$$\operatorname{lcm}(f(x)+f(a)+ax,\, f(x)+f(b)+bx) \mid xf(x) - C.$$
Let $T(x) = \gcd(f(x)+f(a)+ax,\, f(x)+f(b)+bx)$. Since the product of the two factors grows as $x^2$ while $xf(x)$ grows at most as $x^3$, for large $x$ we must have $T(x) \to +\infty$.

Choose $x$ so that $f(a)-f(b)+(a-b)x = d\,p$ where $d = \gcd(a-b, f(a)-f(b))$ and $p$ is a sufficiently large prime (possible by Dirichlet's theorem). Then $p \mid T(x)$. By some calculations,
$$p \mid C(b-a) + (bf(a)-af(b))(f(a)-f(b)),$$
which is a nonzero constant (since $C = af(a) = bf(b)$ with $a > b$ implies $f(b) > f(a)$, so the expression is positive). This is a contradiction for large $p$. $\square$

**Claim 2.** $f$ is non-decreasing.

*Proof.* Suppose $f(x) > f(x+1)$ for some $x$. Let $c = f(x)-f(x+1) > 0$. From $P(c,x)$: $f(c)+f(x)+cx \mid xf(x)-cf(c)$. From $P(c,x+1)$: $f(c)+f(x+1)+c(x+1) = f(c)+f(x)+cx \mid (x+1)f(x+1)-cf(c)$. So $f(c)+f(x)+cx \mid (x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x)$.

If $xf(x) < (x+1)f(x+1)$: then $f(c)+f(x)+cx > (x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x)$, a contradiction.
If $xf(x) > (x+1)f(x+1)$: then $f(x+1)+f(c)+c(x+1) > xf(x)-(x+1)f(x+1)$, a contradiction (since $xf(x)=(x+1)f(x+1)$ is ruled out by Claim 1). $\square$

**Main proof by induction.** Let $k = f(1)-1$. We prove $f(n) = n(n+k)$ by induction.

Base cases $n=1,2$ are handled using Claim 2. Assume $f(i) = i(i+k)$ for all $i \le n$. From $P(n+1,1)$:
$$f(n+1)+n+k+2 \mid (n+1)f(n+1)-(k+1).$$
Write $(n+1)f(n+1)-(k+1) = l(f(n+1)+n+k+2)$ for some positive integer $l < n+1$ (using $f(n+1) \ge f(1) = k+1$ and monotonicity). Then:
$$(k+1)(l+1)+l(n+1) = f(n+1)(n+1-l) \ge f(n)(n+1-l) = n(n+k)(n+1-l).$$
A direct estimate shows $l > n-1$, hence $l = n$, which gives $f(n+1) = (n+1)(n+1+k)$. $\square$

One verifies that $f(n) = n(n+k)$ satisfies the original divisibility: $xf(x)+yf(y)+xy = x^2(x+k)+y^2(y+k)+xy$ and the divisor $xf(x)-yf(y) = x^2(x+k)-y^2(y+k)$ equals $(x-y)(x^2+xy+y^2+k(x+y))$, which is divisible by the corresponding factor.

**Answer:** $f(n) = n^2 + cn$ for some constant $c \in \mathbb{N}_+ \cup \{0\}$.

Fix $y$. Then $f(x)+f(y)+xy \mid x^2y + xf(y) + yf(y)$ (by multiplying the condition through). Call the right-hand side $P_y(x)$. Substituting $y=1$ gives $f(x) \le x^2 + Cx$.

Let $Q_y(x) = \frac{x^2y+xf(y)+yf(y)}{f(x)+f(y)+xy}$. We claim that for fixed $a,b$, the expression $\frac{Q_a(x)}{a} = \frac{Q_b(x)}{b}$ for infinitely many $x$. Using the growth bound $f(x) \ge 0.1 x^{1.997}$ (derived from analyzing $xf(x)$ values), one shows that the difference $\frac{Q_a(x)}{a} - \frac{x^2}{f(x)}$ is small, forcing equality for large $x$.

For infinitely many $x$:
$$\frac{x^2a + xf(a)+af(a)}{a(f(x)+f(a)+xa)} = \frac{x^2b+xf(b)+bf(b)}{b(f(x)+f(b)+xb)}.$$
This means $f(x)$ equals a fixed rational function of $x$, hence a polynomial for infinitely many $x$. Since the numerators are irreducible for all large $a$, we get $f(x)+f(a)+xa = C(x^2+x\frac{f(a)}{a}+f(a))$ for infinitely many $x$, giving:
$$f(x) = Cx^2 + x\frac{Cf(a)-a^2}{a} + (C-1)f(a).$$
From the two equations for $a$ and $b$: either $f(a)=f(b)$ (impossible for a function $\mathbb{N}_+ \to \mathbb{N}_+$ that grows) or $\frac{f(a)}{a}-a = \frac{f(b)}{b}-b$ for all large $a,b$. This gives $f(n) = n^2 + cn$ for all large $n$. Substituting back confirms $C=1$ and that $f(y) = y^2+cy$ for all $y$.

**Answer:** $f(x) = x^2 + cx$ for some non-negative integer $c$.

**Claim 1.** $xf(x)$ is injective.

*Proof.* Suppose $af(a) = bf(b) = c$ with $a > b$. From $P(a,x)$ and $P(b,x)$:
$$\operatorname{lcm}(f(x)+f(a)+ax;\ f(x)+f(b)+bx) \mid xf(x)-c.$$
Let $T(x) = \gcd(f(x)+f(a)+ax;\ f(x)+f(b)+bx)$. Since the product of the two expressions $\gg xf(x)$, we get $T(x) \to \infty$. Choose $x$ by Dirichlet so that $f(a)-f(b)+(a-b)x = dp$ for a large prime $p$. Then $p \mid T(x)$, and by calculation $p \mid C(b-a)+(bf(a)-af(b))(f(a)-f(b))$, a nonzero constant — contradiction for large $p$. $\square$

**Claim 2.** $f$ is non-decreasing.

*Proof.* Suppose $f(x) < f(x+1)$ fails, i.e., suppose $f(x) > f(x+1)$. Let $c = f(x)-f(x+1)$. From $P(x,c)$ and $P(x+1,c)$, the LHS of both divisibility conditions is $f(x)+f(c)+cx$, so this divides $(x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x)$. Checking both cases $xf(x) < (x+1)f(x+1)$ and $xf(x) > (x+1)f(x+1)$ leads to contradictions (the divisor exceeds the dividend in both cases). $\square$

**Claim 3.** $f(x) = ax^2+bx+c$ for infinitely many $x$ (a fixed degree-2 polynomial).

*Proof.* From $P(x,x+1)$: $f(x)+f(x+1)+x(x+1) \mid (x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x)$, giving $f(x) + \frac{f(x+1)}{x+1}+x \le f(x+1)$, so $f(x) \ge 0.49x^2$ for large $x$.

From $P(1,x)$: $f(x)+f(1)+x \mid x^2+xf(1)+f(1)$. For large $x$, the ratio is less than 3, so by pigeonhole some integer value $t \in \{1,2\}$ occurs infinitely often, giving $f(x)$ quadratic for infinitely many $x$. $\square$

**Conclusion.** Write $f(x) = ax^2+bx+c_0$ for infinitely many $x$. Fixing $y$ and using $P(x,y)$ with these $x$ gives a polynomial divisibility in $x$. Working out the polynomial division forces $a=1$, $c_0=0$, so $f(x) = x^2+bx$ for all large $x$, and then for all $x$ by the divisibility condition. $\square$

_audit.gap_note:
Answer f(n)=n^2+kn (k=f(1)-1>=0) is CORRECT and the 'works' direction verifies cleanly: with f(n)=n(n+k), divisor f(x)+f(y)+xy = x^2+xy+y^2+k(x+y), and xf(x)-yf(y)=x^2(x+k)-y^2(y+k)=(x-y)(x^2+xy+y^2+k(x+y)), so the quotient is exactly (x-y). The overall STRATEGY of the best solution (Sol 1, ~= Sol 3) is the known-correct one: (i) injectivity of x*f(x), (ii) f non-decreasing, (iii) pin down f. So approach_sound=true. But there is a genuine load-bearing gap plus circularity, shared by all three reference solutions.

(1) Injectivity (Claim 1) hinges on 'T(x)=gcd(F_a,F_b) -> infinity', justified by 'the product of the two factors grows as x^2 while xf(x) grows at most as x^3.' This is mis-stated and the conclusion is NOT established. With F_a=f(z)+f(a)+az, F_b=f(z)+f(b)+bz and N=zf(z)-C, one has T >= F_a F_b / N. Using only the upper bound f<=z^2+kz one gets T >= f(z)/z + (a+b) + ab z/f(z), and by AM-GM this is >= (sqrt a + sqrt b)^2, a CONSTANT, not ->infinity. T->infinity only follows if f(z) >> z (i.e. f(z) ~ z^2, the lower bound). If f were merely linear, T stays bounded and the whole contradiction (p | fixed nonzero constant) collapses. Since p | T is needed (T | dp, and only T>d forces p|T), the step is essential and unproven as written. (Also a minor algebra slip: the asserted invariant 'C(b-a)+(bf(a)-af(b))(f(a)-f(b))' should be (a-b)^2 C + (bf(a)-af(b))(f(a)-f(b)); spirit is right.)

(2) Circularity. The needed lower bound f(z) >~ z^2 (which would rescue 'T->infinity') is obtained from P(x,x+1): E=f(x)+f(x+1)+x(x+1) | N'=(x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x), and one writes E<=N' to get f(x+1) >= (x+1)/x f(x) + (x+1), hence f >= x^2+kx. But E<=N' requires N'>0, i.e. x*f(x) strictly increasing, which needs monotonicity (Claim 2). Claim 2's proof is rigorous in its two STRICT subcases (xf(x)<(x+1)f(x+1) and >), which I verified: with c=f(x)-f(x+1), both P(c,x) and P(c,x+1) share divisor L=f(c)+f(x)+cx | (x+1)f(x+1)-xf(x)=f(x)-c(x+1), and L exceeds the dividend in both subcases. BUT the remaining subcase xf(x)=(x+1)f(x+1) is dismissed 'by Claim 1' (injectivity) -- which itself needed the lower bound, which needed monotonicity. So Sol 1 is circular at the kernel; Sol 3 has the same defect (its Claim 3 lower bound f>=0.49x^2 silently assumes N'>0, and its Claim 1 'T->infinity' cites 'product >> xf(x)' which again presupposes the lower bound). Sol 2 is the weakest (asserts 'f(x)>=0.1 x^{1.997}', 'by some calculations', 'irreducible for all large a' without proof).

(3) Sol 1's induction also asserts 'a direct estimate shows l>n-1, hence l=n' with no computation -- a second hand-waved load-bearing step.

Partial rigorous progress I did establish (could seed a real fix, but does not close it): From x*D-(xf(x)-yf(y)) one gets D | x^2 y + x f(y) + y f(y); with y=1 this yields the upper bound f(x)<=x^2+kx (k=f(1)-1). Subtracting the symmetric relation and using D|xf(x)-yf(y) gives the clean relation D=f(x)+f(y)+xy | xy(x-y)+x f(y)-y f(x). With y=1 this becomes f(x)+x+k+1 | x^2+kx-f(x), and since f(x)<=x^2+kx the RHS is >=0, giving a DICHOTOMY: for every x, either f(x)=x^2+kx ('exact') or f(x) <= (x^2+(k-1)x-(k+1))/2 (~x^2/2, 'small'). Moreover, taking the same relation with x 'small' and y 'exact' (f(y)=y^2+ky) the RHS collapses to y*(x^2+kx-f(x))=y*delta_x, so D(x,y) | y*delta_x; since D(x,y)>y^2 while y*delta_x is linear in y, choosing an exact y>=delta_x forces a contradiction. Hence: IF there are infinitely many exact values, THEN f(x)=x^2+kx for ALL x. This reduces the whole problem to the single statement 'not all sufficiently large x are small' (equivalently, infinitely many x are exact). I could NOT close that last statement rigorously without re-importing the very injectivity/monotonicity machinery whose justification is the gap, and the cofinite-small case is not contradicted by the elementary relations above. So I did not produce a fully complete corrected proof.

reference_answer: No, such primes and positive integers do not exist.
solutions:
ANSWER: No, such primes and positive integers do not exist.

Suppose for contradiction that primes q,p1,p2 and positive integers m,n,a1,a2 (writing a1=alpha_1, a2=alpha_2) satisfy (1) and (2). Note p1>=2, m>=2 (since m>3n^2>=3), and from p1<p2<p1^{9/8} both p1,p2 are primes; checking p1=2,3,5,7 gives no prime in (p1,p1^{9/8}), while p1=11 gives p2=13, so in fact p1>=11. Also q does not divide p_i: if q|p_i then q=p_i, so q|p_i^m, contradicting q|p_i^m-1. Hence gcd(p_i,q)=1.

Let a=min(a1,a2). Then q^a | q^{a_i} | p_i^m-1, so
   p1^m ≡ 1 and p2^m ≡ 1 (mod q^a).                                    (*)
In particular every product p1^x p2^y (x,y>=0) is a unit mod q^a satisfying (p1^x p2^y)^m ≡ 1.

Lower bound for q^a. From (2), q^{n a1} > p1^{m-1} and q^{n a2} > p2^{m-1} > p1^{m-1}; hence q^{n a} = min(q^{n a1}, q^{n a2}) > p1^{m-1}, i.e.
   q^a > p1^{(m-1)/n}.   Put L := (m-1)/n, so q^a > p1^L.                (**)

The index set. Let T = { (x,y) in Z_{>=0}^2 : x + (9/8) y <= L }. For (x,y) in T, using p2 < p1^{9/8},
   p1^x p2^y <= p1^x (p1^{9/8})^y = p1^{x + (9/8)y} <= p1^L < q^a   by (**).
So every product P(x,y):=p1^x p2^y with (x,y) in T lies in [1, q^a). By unique factorization (p1≠p2 are primes, exponents >=0) the map (x,y) -> P(x,y) is injective into the integers; distinct integers in [1,q^a) have distinct residues mod q^a. Thus the |T| residues P(x,y) mod q^a are pairwise distinct, and by (*) each is a solution of X^m ≡ 1 (mod q^a). Therefore
   |T| <= R, where R := #{ X in (Z/q^a)* : X^m = 1 }.                  (***)

CASE A: q odd. Then (Z/q^a)* is cyclic of order phi(q^a), so the equation X^m=1 has exactly gcd(m, phi(q^a)) <= m solutions: R <= m. We now show |T| > m, contradicting (***).

Count |T|. For each integer y with 0 <= y <= floor(8L/9), the admissible x are 0,1,...,floor(L - (9/8)y), i.e. floor(L-(9/8)y)+1 >= L-(9/8)y values (using floor(t)+1 >= t). With B := floor(8L/9) <= 8L/9, and noting L-(9/8)B >= 0,
   |T| >= sum_{y=0}^{B} (L - (9/8)y) = (B+1)L - (9/8)·B(B+1)/2 = (B+1)(L - (9/16)B).
Since B+1 > 8L/9 and L - (9/16)B >= L - (9/16)(8L/9) = L/2, both factors positive,
   |T| > (8L/9)(L/2) = (4/9) L^2.
Now m > 3n^2 gives n^2 < m/3, so L^2 = (m-1)^2/n^2 > 3(m-1)^2/m, whence
   |T| > (4/9)·3(m-1)^2/m = 4(m-1)^2/(3m).
Finally 4(m-1)^2/(3m) - m = (m^2 - 8m + 4)/(3m) >= 0 for every integer m >= 8 (the quadratic m^2-8m+4 has roots 4±sqrt12 ≈ 0.54, 7.46). Hence for m >= 8 we get |T| > 4(m-1)^2/(3m) >= m, i.e. |T| > m, contradicting |T| <= R <= m.

It remains to treat m <= 7. Since m > 3n^2, this forces n = 1 (n>=2 gives m>12), so L = m-1 and m in {4,5,6,7}. Counting T directly:
   m=4 (L=3): y=0 gives x in {0,1,2,3} (4); y=1 gives x in {0,1} (2); y=2 gives x=0 (1); total |T|=7.
   m=5 (L=4): |T| = 5+3+2+1 = 11.
   m=6 (L=5): |T| = 16.   m=7 (L=6): |T| = 22.
In every case |T| > m, again contradicting |T| <= R <= m. This closes Case A.

CASE B: q = 2. (Here the group is not cyclic, so we do not use (***); instead we use a size/valuation contradiction.) Since 2 | p_i^m - 1, each p_i is odd, p_i >= 11.

If m is odd: by LTE for p=2, v_2(p_i^m - 1) = v_2(p_i - 1). Consider the product p1 itself (x=1,y=0): p1 is a unit mod 2^a with p1^m ≡ 1. When m is odd the only solution of X^m=1 in (Z/2^a)* is X=1 (the group is a 2-group, and an odd power map on a 2-group is a bijection fixing only the identity). Hence p1 ≡ 1 (mod 2^a). But by (**) p1 <= p1^L < 2^a (as L = (m-1)/n >= 1), so 1 <= p1 < 2^a and p1 ≡ 1 forces p1 = 1, impossible. So m is even.

If m is even: by LTE for p=2 (m even, p_i odd),
   v_2(p_i^m - 1) = v_2(p_i^2 - 1) + v_2(m) - 1.
Since 2^{a_i} | p_i^m - 1, we get a_i <= v_2(p_i^2-1) + v_2(m) - 1 < log_2(p_i^2) + log_2 m = 2 log_2 p_i + log_2 m.
On the other hand (2) gives 2^{n a_i} > p_i^{m-1}, i.e. a_i > (m-1) log_2 p_i / n. Combining and dividing by log_2 p_i > 0,
   (m-1)/n < 2 + (log_2 m)/(log_2 p_i) = 2 + log_{p_i} m <= 2 + log_{11} m,
so L < 2 + log_{11} m.
But from m > 3n^2 we have n < sqrt(m/3), hence L = (m-1)/n > sqrt(3)·(m-1)/sqrt(m). Define
   f(m) = sqrt(3)(m-1)/sqrt(m) - 2 - log_{11} m.
Then f(4) = sqrt(3)·3/2 - 2 - log_{11}4 = 2.5981 - 2.5781 = 0.0199 > 0, and f is increasing for m >= 4: its derivative sqrt(3)(1/2)(m^{-1/2}+m^{-3/2}) - 1/(m ln 11) is positive for all m >= 4 (the first term exceeds sqrt(3)/(2 sqrt m) which beats 1/(m ln 11) whenever sqrt(m) > 2/(sqrt(3) ln 11) ≈ 0.48). Hence f(m) > 0 for all m >= 4, i.e. L > sqrt(3)(m-1)/sqrt(m) > 2 + log_{11} m, contradicting L < 2 + log_{11} m above. This closes Case B.

Both cases are impossible, so no such q, p1, p2, m, n, a1, a2 exist. Therefore the answer is NO. ∎
_original_solutions:
No such primes and positive integers exist.

**Key idea (subexponential / smoothness bound, analogous to USEMO 2025/6):**

Assume for contradiction that $q, p_1, p_2, m, n, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$ satisfy all the given conditions. Let $\alpha = \min(\alpha_1, \alpha_2)$.

Since $q^{\alpha_1} \mid p_1^m - 1$ and $q^{\alpha_2} \mid p_2^m - 1$, both $p_1$ and $p_2$ are $m$-th roots of unity modulo $q^{\alpha}$ (i.e., $p_1^m \equiv 1 \pmod{q^{\alpha}}$ and $p_2^m \equiv 1 \pmod{q^{\alpha}}$).

Consequently, for any non-negative integers $a, b$, the product $p_1^a p_2^b$ satisfies $p_1^a p_2^b \equiv p_1^a p_2^b \pmod{q^{\alpha}}$, and in particular products of the form $p_1^a p_2^b$ with $0 \le a, b < m$ give $(p_1^a p_2^b)^m \equiv 1 \pmod{q^\alpha}$, so each such product lies in the subgroup of $m$-th roots mod $q^{\alpha}$, which has order dividing $\gcd(m, \phi(q^\alpha))$ and in particular at most $m$ elements.

Among the products $p_1^a p_2^b$ with $0 \le a, b \le \lfloor \alpha \log_{p_1} q \rfloor$, there are approximately $(\alpha \log_{p_1} q)^2$ distinct integers, all less than $q^{\alpha}$ (since $p_1^a p_2^b < p_2^{2\alpha \log_{p_1} q} \lesssim p_1^{9/4 \cdot \alpha \log_{p_1} q} = q^{9\alpha/4}$; a more careful counting gives the right bound). By pigeonhole, two such products $p_1^{a_1} p_2^{b_1}$ and $p_1^{a_2} p_2^{b_2}$ must be congruent mod $q^\alpha$, giving $p_1^{a_1 - a_2} p_2^{b_1 - b_2} \equiv 1 \pmod{q^\alpha}$. This means the order of $p_1^{|a_1-a_2|} p_2^{|b_1-b_2|}$ divides $m$ and certain exponents are bounded.

From condition (2), $q^{n\alpha_i} > p_i^{m-1}$, so $n\alpha_i > (m-1)\log_{q} p_i$, giving $\alpha_i > \frac{(m-1)}{n} \log_q p_i$. Combined with $m > 3n^2$, this yields $\alpha \log_{p_1} q > \frac{m-1}{n} > 3n - \frac{1}{n} > n$ (for large $n$). Thus the number of products $p_1^a p_2^b$ with $0 \le a, b \le n$ alone is $(n+1)^2 > n^2$ pairs, but by the above they must all be distinct mod $q^\alpha$ within a residue class set of size at most $m$. Expanding using $p_1 < p_2 < p_1^{9/8}$ and the size bounds produces a contradiction: the count $(\alpha \log_{p_1} q)^2$ of small products exceeds $m$, but the $m$-th root subgroup mod $q^\alpha$ can have at most $m$ elements, contradiction.

Therefore, **no** such $q, p_1, p_2, m, n, \alpha_1, \alpha_2$ exist.

_audit.gap_note: Correct answer (No) and correct strategy (the products p1^a p2^b that are < q^alpha are distinct integers yet all m-th roots of unity mod q^alpha, so their number is at most the number of such roots). But the reference is only a sketch: (a) the LOAD-BEARING quantitative step -- that the number of admissible products exceeds m, which is the entire difficulty and is exactly where the constants 9/8 and m>3n^2 are used -- is merely asserted ('produces a contradiction'), never derived; the sketch's own size estimate p1^a p2^b up to q^{9alpha/4} is even self-defeating. (b) It never makes the clean pigeonhole rigorous (distinct integers in [1,q^alpha) have distinct residues, so |T| <= |roots|). (c) The bound 'at most m m-th roots' relies on (Z/q^alpha)* being cyclic, which FAILS for q=2; the q=2 case is entirely ignored (there one can have up to ~2m roots). (d) small m edge cases (n=1, 4<=m<=7) where the loose area bound is insufficient are not addressed. The fix below carries out the exact count (4/9)L^2 > m for m>=8, checks m<=7 by hand, and kills q=2 via LTE + the size condition.

problem:
Let $C > 0$ be a fixed real number. A positive integer $a$ is called 'good' if there exist positive integers $x, y$ such that
$$a = xy \qquad \text{and} \qquad |x - y| \le C \sqrt[4]{a}.$$
List all good numbers in increasing order as
$$a_1 < a_2 < \cdots.$$
Find the smallest real number $\lambda$ for which there exists a positive real number $M$ such that
$$a_{n+1} \le a_n + M a_n^{\lambda}$$
holds for every positive integer $n$.
solutions:
Answer: lambda = 1/2 for 0<C<2, and lambda = 1/4 for C>=2.

Throughout, finitely many initial terms can be absorbed by enlarging the constant M (each a_n^lambda>=1), so it suffices to prove the gap bounds for all sufficiently large n.

=== Claim 1: lambda <= 1/2 for every C>0. ===
For each m define S_m = { m^2 + jm : 0 <= j <= floor(C*sqrt(m)) }. For a = m^2+jm = m(m+j) take x=m, y=m+j; then |x-y| = j <= C*sqrt(m) <= C*a^{1/4} since a >= m^2 gives a^{1/4} >= sqrt(m). So every element of S_m is good. Pick m0 with floor(C*sqrt(m)) >= 3 for m >= m0; then sup S_m = m^2 + m*floor(C*sqrt(m)) >= m^2+3m >= (m+1)^2 = inf S_{m+1}, so the blocks overlap. Consecutive elements inside one block differ by m, and because inf S_{m+1} <= sup S_m, the first element of S_{m+1} above sup S_m is within m+1 of it. Hence the increasing union of all S_m (m>=m0) has consecutive gaps <= m+1, while each element b satisfies b >= m^2, i.e. sqrt(b) >= m. Adding the remaining good numbers only shrinks gaps, so for large n, a_{n+1} <= a_n + 2*a_n^{1/2}. Thus lambda <= 1/2.

=== Claim 2: lambda = 1/2 for 0 < C < 2. ===
Let delta = (4-C^2)/8 in (0,1/2). Fix m > 2C^2/(4-C^2); we show (m^2, m^2+delta*m) contains no good number, so the good number m^2 is followed by a gap >= delta*m = delta*(m^2)^{1/2}. Suppose a=xy is good with m^2 < a <= m^2+delta*m and x<=y. If x>=m+1 then a>=(m+1)^2 = m^2+2m+1 > m^2+delta*m (as delta<2), contradiction; if y<=m then a<=m^2, contradiction. So x=m-u (u>=0), y=m+v (v>=1). Since a>m^2, m(v-u)-uv = a-m^2 > 0 forces v>u, i.e. v-u>=1, hence a-m^2 = m(v-u)-uv >= m-uv. As a < (m+1)^2 we have u+v = y-x <= C*a^{1/4} < C*sqrt(m+1), so by AM-GM uv <= (u+v)^2/4 <= C^2(m+1)/4, giving a-m^2 >= m - C^2(m+1)/4 = ((4-C^2)m - C^2)/4. For m > 2C^2/(4-C^2) this exceeds (4-C^2)m/8 = delta*m, contradicting a <= m^2+delta*m. So infinitely many m give a gap >= delta*a_n^{1/2} at a_n=m^2; any lambda<1/2 would force this gap to be o(a_n^{1/2}), impossible. With Claim 1, lambda = 1/2.

=== Claim 3: lambda >= 1/4 for every C>0 (in particular C>=2). ===
Let G(N) = #{good a <= N}. Each good a<=N has a representation a=xy, x<=y, with x<=sqrt(N) and y in [x, x+C*N^{1/4}]; the number of such pairs is at most sqrt(N)*(C*N^{1/4}+1) <= K*N^{3/4}. So G(N) <= K*N^{3/4}. Suppose, for contradiction, that some M works with lambda<1/4. Since f(x)=x^{1-lambda} is concave (exponent 1-lambda in (3/4,1)), the MVT gives a_{n+1}^{1-lambda}-a_n^{1-lambda} <= (1-lambda)a_n^{-lambda}(a_{n+1}-a_n) <= M(1-lambda). Telescoping from a_1=1, a_n^{1-lambda} <= 1+(n-1)M(1-lambda) <= K3*n, hence a_n <= K3'*n^{1/(1-lambda)}. Therefore a_n <= N whenever n <= (N/K3')^{1-lambda}, so G(N) >= K1*N^{1-lambda} with 1-lambda > 3/4, contradicting G(N) <= K*N^{3/4} for large N. Hence lambda >= 1/4.

=== Claim 4 (corrected): lambda <= 1/4 for C >= 2. ===
We prove that for some t0 every integer t >= t0 has a good number in [t, t+3*t^{1/4}]; this yields a_{n+1} <= a_n + 3*a_n^{1/4} for large n, hence lambda <= 1/4.

Fix t >= t0 and set m = floor(sqrt(t)), so m^2 <= t < (m+1)^2 and sqrt(m) <= t^{1/4} < sqrt(m+1). Define two families of good candidates:
  type 1: g(u) = (m-u)(m+u+1) = m^2 + m - u(u+1),
  type 2: h(u) = (m-u)(m+u+2) = (m+1)^2 - (u+1)^2.

Goodness of type 1 when u(u+1) <= m-1 (so g(u) > m^2): here y-x = 2u+1. Writing w=u(u+1)<=m-1,
  (2u+1)^4 = (4w+1)^2 = 16w^2+8w+1, and 16*g(u) = 16m(m+1)-16w.
  16w^2+8w+1 <= 16m(m+1)-16w  iff  16w^2+24w+1 <= 16m(m+1);
with w<=m-1 the left side is <= 16(m-1)^2+24(m-1)+1 = 16m^2-8m-7 <= 16m^2+16m = 16m(m+1). Hence (2u+1)^4 <= 16*g(u), i.e. 2u+1 <= 2*g(u)^{1/4} <= C*g(u)^{1/4} (using C>=2). So every type-1 number with u(u+1)<=m-1 is good.

Goodness of type 2 when (u+1)^2 <= m (so h(u) > m^2+m): here y-x = 2u+2, and with q=(u+1)^2<=m,
  (2u+2)^4 = 16q^2 and 16*h(u) = 16((m+1)^2 - q);
  16q^2 <= 16((m+1)^2-q)  iff  q(q+1) <= (m+1)^2, true since q(q+1) <= m(m+1) <= (m+1)^2.
Hence 2u+2 <= 2*h(u)^{1/4} <= C*h(u)^{1/4}, so every type-2 number with (u+1)^2<=m is good.

Coverage and gaps on [m^2,(m+1)^2]:
 - Type 1 ranges over g(0)=m^2+m down to g(u1), where u1 is the largest u with u(u+1)<=m-1. Consecutive type-1 gaps are g(u)-g(u+1)=2(u+1) <= 2*sqrt(m)+2 (since u<sqrt(m)). The lowest one satisfies g(u1)-m^2 = m-u1(u1+1); maximality gives (u1+1)(u1+2) >= m, so u1(u1+1) > m-2u1-3, hence g(u1)-m^2 < 2u1+3 <= 2*sqrt(m)+3. So type 1 covers (m^2, m^2+m] with all gaps <= 2*sqrt(m)+3, and its lowest element is within 2*sqrt(m)+3 above m^2.
 - Type 2 ranges over h(0)=m^2+2m down to h(u2), u2 the largest u with (u+1)^2<=m. Gaps h(u)-h(u+1)=2u+3 <= 2*sqrt(m)+3. With q=(u2+1)^2<=m<(u2+2)^2 we get m-q < (u2+2)^2-(u2+1)^2 = 2u2+3, so h(u2)-(m^2+m) = (m+1)^2-q-(m^2+m) = (m+1)-q = (m-q)+1 < 2*sqrt(m)+2. So type 2 covers [m^2+m, m^2+2m] with all gaps <= 2*sqrt(m)+3, its lowest element within 2*sqrt(m)+2 above m^2+m.
 - The squares m^2 and (m+1)^2 = m^2+2m+1 are good.

Now list, in increasing order: m^2; then the type-1 chain g(u1)<...<g(0)=m^2+m; then the type-2 chain (lowest)<...<h(0)=m^2+2m; then (m+1)^2. Every consecutive difference is <= 2*sqrt(m)+3:
   m^2 -> g(u1): <= 2*sqrt(m)+3; inside type 1: <= 2*sqrt(m)+2; g(0)=m^2+m -> lowest type 2: <= 2*sqrt(m)+2; inside type 2: <= 2*sqrt(m)+3; h(0)=m^2+2m -> (m+1)^2: gap 1; and (m+1)^2 starts the type-1 chain of m+1. So the chains link across consecutive m through the squares.

Thus, for large m, the good numbers in [m^2,(m+1)^2] have consecutive gaps <= 2*sqrt(m)+3 <= 3*sqrt(m). For any large t in [m^2,(m+1)^2) this produces a good number in [t, t+3*sqrt(m)] with 3*sqrt(m) <= 3*t^{1/4}. Hence a_{n+1} <= a_n + 3*a_n^{1/4} for large n, so lambda <= 1/4. Combined with Claim 3, lambda = 1/4 for C >= 2.

=== Conclusion ===
lambda = 1/2 for 0 < C < 2, and lambda = 1/4 for C >= 2.
_original_solutions:
**Claim 1:** $\lambda \le \frac{1}{2}$ for any $C > 0$.

For each $m \in \mathbb{N}$, define
$$S_m = \{m^2 + jm : 0 \le j \le \lfloor C\sqrt{m} \rfloor\}.$$
Let $a = m^2 + jm = m(m+j)$. Since $a \ge m^2$ implies $a^{1/4} \ge \sqrt{m}$, we obtain
$$|m - (m+j)| = j \le C\sqrt{m} \le C a^{1/4}.$$
Therefore every element of $S_m$ is a good number.

Because $C > 0$, there exists $m_0$ such that $\lfloor C\sqrt{m} \rfloor \ge 3$ for all $m \ge m_0$. Hence $m^2 + m\lfloor C\sqrt{m} \rfloor \ge m^2 + 3m \ge (m+1)^2$, so $\sup S_m \ge \inf S_{m+1}$.

Two consecutive elements in each $S_m$ differ by exactly $m$. Let $b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$ be the increasing sequence of all numbers in $\bigcup_{m \ge m_0} S_m$. Then $b_{n+1} - b_n \le m$ and since $b_n \ge m^2$ we have $b_n^{1/2} \ge m$. Therefore $b_{n+1} \le b_n + b_n^{1/2}$, giving $\lambda \le \frac{1}{2}$.

**Claim 2:** $\lambda = \frac{1}{2}$ for $0 < C < 2$.

Set $\delta = \frac{4 - C^2}{8} \in (0, \frac{1}{2})$. We show the interval $(m^2, m^2 + \delta m)$ contains no good number. Suppose $a = xy$ is good with $m^2 < a \le m^2 + \delta m$. Write $x = m - u$, $y = m + v$ with $u \ge 0$, $v \ge 1$. Then
$$a - m^2 = m(v - u) - uv \ge m - uv.$$
Since $a$ is good, $u + v = y - x \le C a^{1/4} \le C\sqrt{m+1}$, so by AM-GM,
$$uv \le \frac{(u+v)^2}{4} \le \frac{C^2(m+1)}{4}.$$
Thus $a - m^2 \ge m - \frac{C^2(m+1)}{4} = \frac{(4-C^2)m - C^2}{4} > \delta m$, contradicting $a \le m^2 + \delta m$.

Hence $m^2$ is good and the next good number is at distance $\ge \delta m \ge \delta a_n^{1/2}$ from $m^2$, so $\lambda < \frac{1}{2}$ is impossible. Combined with Claim 1, $\lambda = \frac{1}{2}$.

**Claim 3:** $\lambda \ge \frac{1}{4}$ for $C \ge 2$.

Let $G(N)$ denote the number of good numbers not exceeding $N$. For a good number $a \le N$, write $a = xy$ with $x \le y$. Then $x \le \sqrt{N}$ and $y \in [x, x + CN^{1/4}]$, so $G(N) \le K N^{3/4}$ for some constant $K$.

If $\lambda < \frac{1}{4}$, set $f(x) = x^{1-\lambda}$ (concave). By the MVT,
$$a_{n+1}^{1-\lambda} - a_n^{1-\lambda} \le (1-\lambda) a_n^{-\lambda}(a_{n+1} - a_n) \le M(1-\lambda).$$
Thus $a_n^{1-\lambda} \le 1 + (n-1)(1-\lambda)M$, giving $G(N) \ge K_1 N^{1-\lambda}$ with $1 - \lambda > \frac{3}{4}$, contradicting $G(N) \le K N^{3/4}$. Hence $\lambda \ge \frac{1}{4}$.

**Claim 4:** $\lambda = \frac{1}{4}$ for $C \ge 2$.

Set $R = \lfloor m^{1/4} \rfloor$ and consider
$$T_m = \{(m-r)(m+r+s) : r \in \{0, 1, \ldots, R\},\ s \in \{0, 1, 2\}\}.$$
For any $a \in T_m$, we have $|(m+r+s) - (m-r)| = 2r + s \le 2m^{1/4} + 2 \le C a^{1/4}$ for large $m$, so $a$ is good. Within $T_m$, consecutive elements (increasing $r$ by 1) differ by at most $2m^{1/4} + 3 \le 3m^{1/4}$. Also $m(m+2) \in T_m$ and $(m+1)^2 \in T_{m+1}$ are distance 1 apart. Since $a^{1/4} \ge (m^2/2)^{1/4} \ge 3m^{1/4}$ for large $m$, any two consecutive elements of $\bigcup_m T_m$ differ by at most $3m^{1/4} \le a^{1/4}$, giving $a_{n+1} \le a_n + M a_n^{1/4}$ for large $n$. Hence $\lambda \le \frac{1}{4}$, so $\lambda = \frac{1}{4}$.

**Conclusion:**
$$\lambda = \begin{cases} \dfrac{1}{2}, & 0 < C < 2, \\ \dfrac{1}{4}, & C \ge 2. \end{cases}$$

_audit.gap_note: Answer correct (lambda=1/2 for 0<C<2, lambda=1/4 for C>=2) and Claims 1-3 are fine. Claim 4 (upper bound lambda<=1/4 for C>=2) is BROKEN. It sets R=floor(m^{1/4}), so each block T_m is three clusters of width ~R^2~sqrt(m) sitting near m^2, m^2+m, m^2+2m. Between the s=0 cluster (top m^2) and the s=1 cluster (bottom ~m^2+m-sqrt(m)) there is a gap of size ~m~a^{1/2}, NOT 3*m^{1/4}; e.g. at m=10^8 the gap is ~10^8 while the claimed bound is 300. So the load-bearing statement 'consecutive elements of union T_m differ by at most 3m^{1/4}' is false and the upper bound is not established. The cure (used in fixed_solution) is to let the parameter u run all the way up to ~sqrt(m), which keeps y-x=2u+O(1)~2sqrt(m) <= C*a^{1/4} precisely because C>=2; this fills the whole interval (m^2,(m+1)^2) with good numbers spaced O(sqrt(m))=O(a^{1/4}) apart. Verified numerically: (2u+1)^4<=16 g(u) whenever u(u+1)<=m-1, and (2u+2)^4<=16 h(u) whenever (u+1)^2<=m, with no counterexamples over 200000 random trials.

problem:
Let triangle $ABC$ have side lengths $a, b, c$ and circumcircle $\Gamma$. For a point $P \in \Gamma$, denote by $\ell_P$ the Simson line of $P$ with respect to triangle $ABC$. Define the set
$$S = \{\ell_{P_1} \cap \ell_{P_2} : P_1, P_2 \in \Gamma,\ P_1 \neq P_2\}.$$
Find the area of the smallest convex set containing $S$ in terms of $a, b, c$.
reference_answer: $\dfrac{\pi a^2 b^2 c^2}{64\Delta^2}$, where $\Delta$ is the area of triangle $ABC$; equivalently $\dfrac{\pi a^2 b^2 c^2}{4(a+b+c)(b+c-a)(a+c-b)(a+b-c)}$
solutions:
Setup (complex coordinates). Put the circumcircle Gamma as |z| = R with circumcenter O at the origin; let A=a, B=b, C=c be the vertices on Gamma and H = a+b+c the orthocenter. For P = p on Gamma, the foot of the perpendicular from p onto line BC is (1/2)(b + c + p - bc/p) (using p\bar p = R^2, here normalized R=1 for the geometry; we restore R at the end). Hence the three feet are F_a=(1/2)(b+c+p-bc/p), F_b=(1/2)(a+c+p-ac/p), F_c=(1/2)(a+b+p-ab/p), and the Simson line l_p of p is the line through them. A direct subtraction gives the direction F_a - F_b = (1/2)(b-a)(p-c)/p, so l_p has a well-defined direction depending on p.

Step 1: the Simson line passes through a point of the nine-point circle. It is classical (and follows from the feet formulas) that l_p bisects the segment PH; thus l_p passes through M_p = (p+H)/2. Translating so the nine-point center N = H/2 is the origin, M_p = p/2 lies on the circle |w| = R/2 (the nine-point circle).

Step 2: the envelope of the Simson lines is the Steiner deltoid. Writing p = R e^{i theta}, the computation arg(direction) = arg(b-a) + arg(p-c) - arg(p) shows that as theta increases the orientation of l_p decreases at exactly half the rate (the line orientation rotates by -pi as P goes once around Gamma). With N as origin and a suitable fixed rotation of axes, the family of Simson lines is exactly the family of tangent lines of the curve z(t) = a0(2 e^{i t} + e^{-2 i t}),  a0 = R/2, the Steiner deltoid (a three-cusped hypocycloid). Its tangent at parameter t has the equation T_t:  x sin(t/2) + y cos(t/2) = a0 sin(3t/2). The deltoid's inscribed circle is the nine-point circle (radius a0 = R/2); its cusps occur where z'(t)=0, i.e. e^{3 i t}=1, t = 0, 2pi/3, 4pi/3, giving the three cusp points 3 a0 e^{i 2 pi k /3} = (3R/2) e^{i 2 pi k/3}, k=0,1,2 — exactly 120 deg apart on the circle of radius 3R/2 about N.

Step 3: intersection of two Simson lines. Two distinct Simson lines are two tangents T_{t1}, T_{t2}. Solving the two linear equations (let s_j = t_j/2, sigma = s1+s2, delta = s1-s2) one gets, after the product-to-sum simplifications,
  w / a0 = 2 cos(delta) e^{i sigma} + e^{-2 i sigma}.
Indeed x/a0 = cos 2 sigma + 2 cos sigma cos delta and y/a0 = - sin 2 sigma + 2 sin sigma cos delta, which combine to the boxed formula. (As t1 -> t2, delta -> 0 and w -> a0(2 e^{i sigma} + e^{-2 i sigma}) = z(sigma), the point of tangency, as it must.)

Step 4: the locus S and its convex hull. As (t1, t2) range over distinct pairs, sigma ranges over all of [0, 2pi) and cos delta ranges over [-1, 1]. From w/a0 = 2 cos(delta) e^{i sigma} + e^{-2 i sigma} we get |w| <= a0(2|cos delta| + 1) <= 3 a0 = 3R/2, with equality only when cos delta = +-1 and at the cusp parameters; the three farthest points are precisely the three cusps V_k = (3R/2) e^{i 2 pi k/3} (k=0,1,2). Thus S is contained in the closed disk of radius 3R/2 and, more precisely, every intersection point lies in the equilateral triangle T with vertices V_0,V_1,V_2: for each fixed sigma the point w traces, as cos delta runs over [-1,1], the segment between z(sigma) and a0(-2 e^{i sigma}+e^{-2 i sigma}), and the support function h(psi) = max_w Re(w e^{-i psi}) = a0 max_sigma (2|cos(sigma-psi)| + cos(2 sigma + psi)) equals the support function of T (its maxima are attained exactly at the three cusps). Hence the closed convex hull of S equals T. (The three cusps are limit points of S, attained as t1 -> t2 at a cusp parameter, so the smallest convex set containing S is T.) The deltoid arcs between consecutive cusps are concave toward N, so they lie inside T and contribute nothing beyond it.

Step 5: area. T is the equilateral triangle inscribed in the circle of radius rho = 3R/2. An equilateral triangle with circumradius rho has area (3 sqrt 3 / 4) rho^2, so
  Area = (3 sqrt 3 / 4)(3R/2)^2 = 27 sqrt 3 R^2 / 16.
Using R = abc/(4 Delta) with Delta = sqrt(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)) the area of triangle ABC (so R^2 = a^2 b^2 c^2 /(16 Delta^2)),
  Area = 27 sqrt 3 a^2 b^2 c^2 / (256 Delta^2)
      = 27 sqrt 3 a^2 b^2 c^2 / (16 (a+b+c)(b+c-a)(a+c-b)(a+b-c)).
Verification: for a unit circumcircle (R=1) this gives 27 sqrt 3 /16 = 2.92284, matching the directly computed convex-hull area of the set of Simson-line intersections (2.9228), and the three extreme directions are 120 deg apart at radius 3/2 = 3R/2, confirming the triangle and refuting the nine-point-disk claim (pi/4 = 0.785).
_original_solutions:
We use the key fact that for two distinct points $P_1, P_2$ on the circumcircle $\Gamma$, the intersection of their Simson lines $\ell_{P_1} \cap \ell_{P_2}$ lies on the nine-point circle of triangle $ABC$. As $P_1, P_2$ range over all pairs of distinct points on $\Gamma$, the set $S$ fills the nine-point circle (every point on the nine-point circle is realised as such an intersection). The nine-point circle has radius $R/2$ where $R$ is the circumradius, and the smallest convex set containing a circle is the closed disk bounded by it.

We use the standard relation $R = \dfrac{abc}{4\Delta}$ where $\Delta = \sqrt{s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}$, $s = \dfrac{a+b+c}{2}$.

The area of the nine-point disk is
$$\mathcal{A} = \pi\left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi R^2}{4} = \frac{\pi a^2 b^2 c^2}{64\Delta^2} = \frac{\pi a^2 b^2 c^2}{4(a+b+c)(b+c-a)(a+c-b)(a+b-c)}.$$

_audit.gap_note: The reference solution is built on a FALSE lemma: it claims 'for two distinct points P1,P2 on the circumcircle, the intersection l_{P1} \cap l_{P2} lies on the nine-point circle, and as the pair ranges over Gamma the set S fills the nine-point circle.' This is false. The true classical fact is far weaker: only the Simson lines of a PAIR OF ANTIPODAL points are perpendicular and meet on the nine-point circle. For general pairs the intersection does NOT lie on the nine-point circle. The correct object is the Steiner deltoid: the Simson lines envelope a hypocycloid of three cusps (the Steiner deltoid) whose inscribed circle is the nine-point circle (center N = nine-point center, radius R/2) and whose three cusps lie 120 deg apart on the circle of center N and radius 3R/2. The pairwise intersections of Simson lines (= intersections of pairs of tangents to this deltoid) fill the deltoid's region and reach the three cusps; the smallest convex set containing S is therefore the equilateral triangle whose vertices are the three cusps, NOT the nine-point disk. Numerically (unit circumcircle): intersections reach distance 1.5 = 3R/2 from N with three extreme directions exactly 120 deg apart, and the convex hull area is 2.92284 = 27*sqrt(3)/16, whereas the reference's pi R^2/4 = 0.785 is wrong both in value and in shape (a triangle, not a disk; the answer should not contain pi). Correct area = 27 sqrt(3) R^2/16 = 27 sqrt(3) a^2 b^2 c^2 / (256 Delta^2).

solutions:
Setup. Coins have integer values v_1,...,v_m >= 1 with sum S; S is taken large.

Step 1 (dyadic pigeonhole). For j >= 0 let bucket B_j be the coins with value in [2^j, 2^{j+1}). All values lie in [1,S], so only j <= log_2 S give nonempty buckets, i.e. at most floor(log_2 S)+1 buckets. Since the buckets partition the coins, some bucket has total value >= S/(floor(log_2 S)+1). Fix it, write K = 2^j, so its coins have values in [K,2K), and let n be their count and T their total value. Then T < 2nK and T >= S/(floor(log_2 S)+1). For S large, floor(log_2 S)+1 <= S^{0.0064}, hence T >= S^{0.9936} and 2nK > S^{0.9936}.

Step 2 (small bucket). If n <= 100, select ALL coins of this bucket as a SINGLE pile (allowed: 'possibly one pile', <=100 coins). With one pile the equal-count and equal-value conditions hold trivially, and the total value is T >= S^{0.9936} > S^{0.98} for S large. Done. Henceforth assume n >= 100.

Step 3 (common-sum 100-subsets). Consider the C(n,100) subsets of 100 coins of the bucket. Each has value-sum in [100K, 200K), at most 100K distinct integer values. By pigeonhole some value V is the sum of at least C(n,100)/(100K) of these subsets. Let F be this family: distinct 100-element sets, all of sum V. Using C(n,100) >= (n/100)^{100},
  |F| >= (n/100)^{100}/(100K) = n^{100}/(100^{100} * 100 K).

Step 4 (sunflower lemma). Erdos-Rado: any family of MORE THAN s!(k-1)^s distinct s-element sets contains a k-sunflower (k sets with a common core C and pairwise disjoint petals A_i \ C). Here s = 100. Take t to be the largest integer with |F| > 100!(t-1)^{100}; then t-1 < (|F|/100!)^{1/100}, so a t-sunflower exists in F with
  t >= (|F|/100!)^{1/100} >= n / (100^{1.01} (100!)^{1/100}) * K^{-0.01} =: c_1 * n * K^{-0.01},
where c_1 = 100^{-1.01}(100!)^{-1/100} > 0 is an absolute constant.

Step 5 (build the piles). Let A_1,...,A_t be the sunflower, core C, petals P_i = A_i \ C. The petals are pairwise disjoint, all of size 100 - |C|, and since every A_i has sum V we get sum(P_i) = V - sum(C), the SAME for every i. Because the A_i are distinct 100-sets, |C| <= 99, so each petal is nonempty; each of its coins has value >= K, hence sum(P_i) >= K. Select the union of the petals and use the P_i as piles: there are t piles, each with 100 - |C| <= 100 coins (equal counts), each of equal value V - sum(C). The total selected value is
  t * (V - sum(C)) >= tK >= c_1 * n * K^{0.99}.

Step 6 (final estimate). Since n >= 1, n K^{0.99} = (nK)^{0.99} * n^{0.01} >= (nK)^{0.99}. From 2nK > S^{0.9936} we have nK > S^{0.9936}/2, so
  total value >= c_1 (nK)^{0.99} > c_1 (S^{0.9936}/2)^{0.99} = (c_1 / 2^{0.99}) S^{0.983664}.
Because 0.983664 > 0.98, for all sufficiently large S the right side exceeds S^{0.98}. Hence there is S_0 such that for every S >= S_0 the required selection exists. QED.
_original_solutions:
**Lemma (Erdős–Rado Sunflower Lemma).** Given a family $\mathcal{F}$ of more than $s!(k-1)^s$ sets each of size $s$, there must exist $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_k \in \mathcal{F}$ such that
$$A_i \cap A_j = A_u \cap A_v, \quad \forall (i, j, u, v) \in [k]^4.$$

**Proof of main problem.** By Pigeonhole, there exists a number $K$ such that
$$\sum_{K \le \text{coin} \le 2K} \text{coin} \ge \frac{S}{\lceil \log_2(S) \rceil + 1} > S^{0.9936}.$$
Therefore $2nK > S^{0.9936}$, where $n$ is the number of coins with value in $[K, 2K]$.

Assume $n > 100$ (otherwise the single largest-value coin or a direct pile can be handled). By Pigeonhole, among all $\binom{n}{100}$ subsets of 100 coins from this range, at least
$$\frac{\binom{n}{100}}{100K} > \frac{c\, n^{100}}{K}$$
of them share the same sum of elements (since all such sums lie in $[100K, 200K]$).

Applying the Sunflower Lemma to this family of $100$-element sets, we obtain at least
$$t = \frac{c_1 n}{K^{0.01}}$$
sets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_t$ all sharing the same sum, and satisfying $S_i \cap S_j = S_m \cap S_p$ for all $i,j,m,p$.

Erasing the common "core" elements from each $S_i$ yields piles of equal size and equal sum. The total value of the selected coins is at least
$$K \cdot t = c_1 n K^{0.99} = \frac{c_1}{2^{0.99}}(2nK)^{0.99} \cdot n^{0.01} > \frac{c_1}{2^{0.99}} \cdot S^{0.9936 \cdot 0.99} \cdot n^{0.01} > S^{0.98}.$$
Hence for sufficiently large $S$, the construction works and the selected coins exceed $S^{0.98}$ in total value. $\blacksquare$

_audit.gap_note: The strategy (dyadic pigeonhole to find a value-bucket [K,2K) with 2nK > S^0.9936; pigeonhole on the sums of 100-element subsets to get many subsets of a common sum; Erdos-Rado sunflower lemma to extract a sunflower; delete the common core so the disjoint petals become equal-size, equal-sum piles of size <=100; final exponent arithmetic 0.9936*0.99 = 0.983664 > 0.98) is entirely correct and all key steps are present and valid. Two minor blemishes keep it from a clean 'ok': (1) the n<=100 case is dismissed with the confusing phrase 'the single largest-value coin or a direct pile can be handled' -- a single largest coin need NOT exceed S^0.98; the correct (and trivial) handling is to take the whole bucket as ONE pile (allowed: 'possibly one pile'), whose total > S^0.9936 > S^0.98. (2) Constants and the existence of a t-sunflower with t >= c1 n K^{-0.01} are asserted without tracking; one should verify t is taken as the largest k with |F| > 100!(k-1)^{100}, giving t >= (|F|/100!)^{1/100}, and that the petals are nonempty (|C| <= 99 since the sets are distinct). All gaps are routine to fill and do not threaten correctness.

problem:
Two integers $a$ and $b$ are called **companions** if every prime number $p$ either divides both or none of $a, b$. Determine all functions $f : \mathbb{N}_0 \to \mathbb{N}_0$ such that $f(0) = 0$ and the numbers $f(m) + n$ and $f(n) + m$ are companions for all $m, n \in \mathbb{N}_0$.

(Here $\mathbb{N}_0$ denotes the set of all non-negative integers.)

Two decks $A$ and $B$ of 40 cards each are placed on a table at noon. Every minute thereafter, we pick the top cards $a \in A$ and $b \in B$ and perform a duel.

For any two cards $a \in A$ and $b \in B$, each time $a$ and $b$ duel, the outcome remains the same and is independent of all other duels. A duel has three possible outcomes:
- If $a$ card wins, it is placed back at the top of its deck and the losing card is placed at the bottom of its deck.
- If $a$ and $b$ are evenly matched, they are both removed from their respective decks.
- If $a$ and $b$ do not interact with each other, then both are placed at the bottom of their respective decks.

The process ends when both decks are empty. A process is called a game if it ends.

Prove that the maximum time a game can last equals 356 hours.

In triangle $ABC$, points $D, E, F$ lie on sides $BC, AC, AB$ such that
$$BD = DE = EA \quad \text{and} \quad CD = DF = FA.$$
Let $BE$ and $CF$ meet at $P$. Prove that the orthocenter of $\triangle PEF$ lies on $BC$.
solutions:
Let $l_B$ be the reflection of line $BC$ across $BP$ and let $l_C$ be the reflection of line $BC$ across $CP$. Observe $l_B \parallel DE$ and $l_C \parallel DF$. Hence
$$\measuredangle(l_B, l_C) = \measuredangle(DE, DF) = -\measuredangle(AE, AF) = \measuredangle(AB, AC).$$
So $X = l_B \cap l_C$ lies on $(ABC)$. Now we claim that line $AX$ (if $X = A$ then this is a tangent to the circumcircle) is the reflection of line $BC$ across $EF$. This follows from the fact that $A$ is the reflection of $D$ across $EF$ and that
$$\measuredangle XAF = \measuredangle XCB = \measuredangle FDB = -\measuredangle BDF.$$
Now we know that the reflections of $X$ across the sides of $\triangle PEF$ are collinear along $BC$, so $X \in (PEF)$ and $BC$ is the $X$-Steiner line. It is a well-known property that the orthocenter of $\triangle PEF$ lies on the Steiner line of $X$ with respect to $\triangle PEF$, so we are finished.

Start by observing that $A$ and $D$ are reflections in $EF$. Let the perpendicular bisector of $CF$ meet $EF$ at $I_b$, and define $I_c$ similarly. Since $C$ and $F$ are reflections in $DI_b$, we have
$$\angle BCI_b = \angle DFI_b = \angle AFI_b = 180^\circ - \angle BFI_b,$$
hence $BCI_bF$ is cyclic, and so is $BCI_cE$ by symmetry. Let $T = \overline{EF} \cap \overline{BC}$. Since $TI_b \cdot TF = TB \cdot TC = TI_c \cdot TE$, we have $TI_b / TE = TI_c / TF$. Hence there exists a homothety $\Phi$ centered at $T$ sending $I_b \mapsto E$ and $I_c \mapsto F$.

Let $Q = \Phi(D)$. Since $DI_b \perp CF$ and $DI_c \perp BE$, we have $QE \perp CF$ and $QF \perp BE$. Thus $Q$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle PEF$. Since $Q = \Phi(D)$ and $D \in BC$, and $\Phi$ is centered at $T \in BC$, we conclude $Q \in BC$, solving the problem.

**Step 1: Key constraint.** We first establish $AB + AC = 2BC$. Let $K, L$ be the feet of the perpendicular from $D$ to $AB$ and $AC$ respectively (i.e., the line through $D$ perpendicular to $AD$ meets $AB$ at $K$ and $AC$ at $L$). Then $DC = DF = FA = FK$ and $BD = DE = EA = EL$. By Menelaus' theorem on $\triangle ABC$ with transversal $K, L, D$:
$$\frac{BK}{KA} \cdot \frac{AL}{LC} \cdot \frac{CD}{DB} = 1.$$
Since $KA = 2CD$ and $AL = 2DB$, we get $BK = LC$, and then $AB + AC = AK + AL = 2DC + 2DB = 2BC$.

**Step 2: Incenter concyclicity.** Let $I$ be the incenter of $\triangle ABC$. Since $AB + AC = 2BC$, the contact lengths satisfy $AN = AM = BC/2 = (AE + AF)/2$, so $FN = ME$. This gives $\triangle FNI \cong \triangle EMI$, hence $IF = IE$ and $\angle FIE = 180^\circ - \angle BAC$, proving $A, F, I, E$ are concyclic.

**Step 3: Angle computation.** From the concyclicity and angle chasing: $\angle PFE = \angle ABC/2$ and $\angle PEF = \angle ACB/2$.

**Step 4: Locating the orthocenter.** Let $H$ be the second intersection of $(BFI)$ with $BC$. Since $\angle FBI = \angle HBI$, we get $IF = IH$. But from Step 2, $IF = IE$, so $IE = IH$, meaning $C, E, I, H$ are concyclic. Thus $I$ is the circumcenter of $\triangle HEF$.

Since $\angle FEH = \angle FIH/2 = 90^\circ - \angle ABC/2 = 90^\circ - \angle PFE$, it follows that $FP \perp EH$. By symmetry, $EP \perp FH$. Therefore $H$ is the orthocenter of $\triangle PEF$, and $H$ lies on $BC$.

_audit.gap_note: none. Solution 2 is fully rigorous. Configuration facts verified: EF is the perpendicular bisector of AD (E,F equidistant from A,D via EA=ED, FA=FD), so A,D reflect in EF. Line DI_b is the perpendicular bisector of CF (DC=DF), giving the reflection C<->F. The angle chase ∠BCI_b=∠DCI_b=∠DFI_b=∠AFI_b=180°-∠BFI_b correctly establishes BCI_bF cyclic (and BCI_cE by the B<->C, E<->F symmetry). Since I_b,I_c,E,F all lie on line EF, the power of T=EF∩BC gives TI_b·TF=TB·TC=TI_c·TE, so signed ratios match and the homothety Φ at T with I_b↦E, I_c↦F is well-defined. As DI_b⊥CF and DI_c⊥BE, their images QE,QF are perpendicular to CF (=line PF) and BE (=line PE), making Q=Φ(D) the orthocenter of PEF. Finally Φ is centered at T∈BC and D∈BC, so Q∈BC. All steps justified.

problem:
For a prime $p > 7$ we define:
$$S_p = \{1, 2, 3, \ldots, p(p-1)\}$$
Find all functions $f : S_p \to S_p$ such that for all $c \in \{0, 1, \ldots, p-1\}$ there exists $a \in S_p$ such that $f(a) \equiv c \pmod{p}$, and also for all $m, n \in S_p$ we have:
$$(mf(n))^{f(n+(p-1)m)} \equiv f(mn)^n \pmod{p}$$

**Note:** The input of $f$ is always considered $\pmod{p(p-1)}$. For example if $p = 11$, then $f(120) = f(10)$.
reference_answer: $f(n) \equiv (p - c(p-1))n \pmod{p(p-1)}$ for any constant $c \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$, and additionally $f(n) \equiv \begin{cases} (p-c(p-1))n \pmod{p(p-1)} & \text{if } n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p} \\ (p-c(p-1))n + \frac{p(p-1)}{2} \pmod{p(p-1)} & \text{if } n \equiv 0 \pmod{p} \end{cases}$ where $c$ is any nonzero quadratic residue mod $p$.
solutions:
Throughout, identify Z/p(p-1) with Z/p x Z/(p-1) by CRT (gcd(p,p-1)=1); all values of f and all inputs are read mod p(p-1). Write a==b for congruence.

ANSWER (corrected). The solutions are exactly the functions f for which there is a constant c, c != 0 (mod p), with
  (i) f(n) == c n (mod p) for ALL n, and
  (ii) f(n) == n (mod p-1) for all n with n != 0 (mod p),
and on the inputs n == 0 (mod p):
  - if c is a quadratic NON-residue mod p, then f(n) == n (mod p-1) (forced);
  - if c is a quadratic residue mod p, then for EACH residue class n == 0 (mod p) one may independently take either f(n) == n (mod p-1) or f(n) == n + (p-1)/2 (mod p-1).
In CRT-recombined form: f(n) == (p - c(p-1)) n (mod p(p-1)) for n != 0 (mod p); and for n == 0 (mod p), f(n) == (p - c(p-1)) n + delta_n * p(p-1)/2 (mod p(p-1)), where delta_n = 0 always when c is a non-residue, and delta_n in {0,1} is arbitrary (per input class) when c is a residue. The reference's two displayed families are the special cases delta_n == 0 (linear family) and delta_n == 1 for ALL multiples of p (one particular QR function); the remaining 'mixed' choices were omitted.

PROOF.

Step 1 (reduction mod p and bijectivity). Put m == 0 (mod p). Then m f(n) == 0, so the LHS is 0^{f(...)} = 0 (the exponent f(...) is a positive integer). The RHS is f(mn)^n with mn == 0 (mod p), so f(mn) == 0 (mod p) for every input that is a multiple of p. Hence f(x) == 0 (mod p) whenever x == 0 (mod p). Next replace m by m+p. The base (m+p)f(n) == m f(n) (mod p) is unchanged, and the exponent f(n+(p-1)(m+p)) = f(n+(p-1)m + p(p-1)) = f(n+(p-1)m) is unchanged because inputs are read mod p(p-1). So the LHS is unchanged while the RHS becomes f((m+p)n)^n; taking n=1 gives f(m) == f(m+p) (mod p). Thus x -> f(x) mod p depends only on x mod p; being surjective mod p (hypothesis) it induces a bijection of Z/p that fixes 0.

Claim 1: f(n) == c n (mod p) for a fixed c != 0 (mod p). Fix n != 0; then f(n) != 0. Choose m == 1/f(n) (mod p), so m f(n) == 1 and the LHS is 1. Hence f(mn)^n == 1 (mod p) where mn == n/f(n) (mod p). Comparing the inputs n and n+p (which have the same residue n/f(n) mod p, so the same value A := f(mn) mod p): A^n == 1 and A^{n+p} == 1, so A^p == 1; by Fermat A^p == A, hence A == 1. Thus f(n/f(n)) == 1 (mod p). The bijection of Step 1 has a unique input with value 1, so n/f(n) is a constant d (mod p); set c = 1/d. Then f(n) == c n (mod p) for n != 0, and also for n == 0 since f(0) == 0. []

Main relation. Define h(x) := f(x) - x (mod p-1). For m, n both != 0 (mod p), the base m f(n) == c m n != 0 (mod p), and the RHS is f(mn)^n == (cmn)^n. The exponent reduces mod p-1: f(n+(p-1)m) == (n+(p-1)m) + h(n+(p-1)m) == n + h(n+(p-1)m) (mod p-1), since (p-1)m == 0 (mod p-1). So
   (cmn)^{n + h(n+(p-1)m)} == (cmn)^n (mod p),
and cancelling (cmn)^n gives
   ord_p(cmn) | h(n+(p-1)m).         (*)

Reparametrisation. Fix any input x and let r := x mod p. Choose n with n == x (mod p-1) and n mod p arbitrary (possible by CRT, since gcd(p,p-1)=1), subject to n != 0 (mod p); set m := n - r (mod p). Put v := n + (p-1)m. Then v == n == x (mod p-1) and v == n - m == r == x (mod p), so v == x (mod p(p-1)), i.e. v = x. Crucially x is fixed while n mod p (hence m == n - r) is free. With cmn == c n (n - r) (mod p), (*) yields
   ord_p( c n (n - r) ) | h(x)  for every n != 0, r (mod p).   (**)

Claim 3: for constants c, t != 0 (mod p), lcm over n != 0, -t of ord_p(c n (n+t)) = p-1. Suppose not; the lcm is some k | (p-1), k < p-1, so k <= (p-1)/2 and c^k n^k (n+t)^k == 1 for all n != 0, -t. Multiplying by (n+t): c^k x^k (x+t)^{k+1} == (x+t) for x != 0 (at x = -t both sides are 0, so it also holds there); only x = 0 is excluded. Hence the polynomial c^k x^k (x+t)^{k+1} - (x+t) vanishes at all p-1 nonzero residues, so x^{p-1} - 1 divides it in F_p[x], giving
   c^k x^k (x+t)^{k+1} == (x+t)  (mod x^{p-1} - 1).
The right side has exactly 2 nonzero terms (exponents 0 and 1). We show the left side, reduced mod x^{p-1}-1, has more than 2, a contradiction. Expand: x^k (x+t)^{k+1} = sum_{i=0}^{k+1} C(k+1,i) t^{k+1-i} x^{k+i}, with exponents k, k+1, ..., 2k+1 (that is k+2 distinct exponents) and ALL coefficients nonzero (t != 0 and C(k+1,i) != 0 mod p because k+1 <= (p+1)/2 < p). Reduction mod x^{p-1}-1 sends x^e to x^{e mod (p-1)}.
  - If 2k+1 <= p-2 (no exponent reaches p-1): the reduction is the identity, so there remain k+2 >= 3 nonzero terms. Contradiction.
  - The only other case is k = (p-1)/2, where exponents run k=(p-1)/2,...,2k+1=p. Reduction fixes the exponents (p-1)/2,...,p-2, sends p-1 -> 0 and p -> 1. Since p>7, (p-1)/2 >= 3 > 1, so the original expansion has NO term at exponent 0 or 1; thus the wrapped terms (exponents 0,1) cannot cancel anything, and the (p-1)/2 unwrapped terms at exponents (p-1)/2,...,p-2 survive. Total >= (p-1)/2 + 2 >= 5 nonzero terms. Contradiction.
Hence the lcm equals p-1. []

Resolving h.
(a) x != 0 (mod p): here r = x mod p != 0; take t = -r in Claim 3 applied to (**): lcm_n ord_p(c n (n - r)) = p-1, so (p-1) | h(x), i.e. h(x) == 0 (mod p-1). Therefore f(x) == c x (mod p) and f(x) == x (mod p-1).
(b) x == 0 (mod p): here r = 0, so (**) gives ord_p(c n^2) | h(x) for all n != 0, i.e. (lcm_{n != 0} ord_p(c n^2)) | h(x). As n ranges over nonzero residues, c n^2 ranges over c * (nonzero quadratic residues).
   - If c is a non-residue: c * QR = the set of non-residues, which contains the primitive roots (every generator is a non-residue), so the lcm is p-1 and h(x) == 0 (mod p-1).
   - If c is a residue: c * QR = the nonzero quadratic residues, the unique index-2 subgroup of (Z/p)^*, which is cyclic of order (p-1)/2; the lcm of the orders of its elements is (p-1)/2 (it contains a generator of that subgroup). So (p-1)/2 | h(x), giving h(x) in {0, (p-1)/2} (mod p-1). Note this constraint is on EACH input residue x == 0 (mod p) separately; nothing links the different multiple-of-p classes, so the choice is independent for each.

This shows every solution has the stated form. 

Sufficiency (these all work). Let f satisfy (i),(ii) above with some delta-choice. The FE is checked mod p by cases.
  - If m == 0 or n == 0 (mod p): then mn == 0 (mod p), and m f(n) == c m n == 0 (mod p) likewise; both sides equal 0^{positive} = 0.
  - If m, n != 0 (mod p): base c m n != 0; we need (cmn)^{h(v)} == 1 with v = n+(p-1)m, v == n - m (mod p). If v != 0 (mod p) then h(v) = 0 and the factor is 1. If v == 0 (mod p), i.e. m == n (mod p), then cmn == c n^2 (mod p) and h(v) is 0 or (p-1)/2. When h(v)=0 it is trivial; when h(v)=(p-1)/2 we need (c n^2)^{(p-1)/2} == 1, which holds precisely because c is a quadratic residue (so c n^2 is a quadratic residue). This is why the (p-1)/2 shifts are admissible only for QR c, and why they may be applied to ANY subset of the multiple-of-p classes (the factor (c n^2)^{(p-1)/2} = 1 regardless of which v is shifted). Surjectivity mod p holds since adding p(p-1)/2 (== 0 mod p) leaves f mod p == c n unchanged, and c != 0 makes n -> c n surjective.

This completes the determination of all solutions. (Verified computationally for p=11: with c=3 (a QR) the FE and surjectivity hold for the linear function, for the all-shifted function, for the function shifting only the class n==11, and for shifting an arbitrary 4-element subset of the multiple-of-p classes; with c=2 (a non-residue) any nonzero shift breaks the FE.)
_original_solutions:
The functions which work are
$$f(n) \equiv (p - c(p-1))n \pmod{p(p-1)}$$
for any constant $c \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$, as well as
$$f(n) \equiv \begin{cases} (p-c(p-1))n \pmod{p(p-1)} & \text{if } n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p} \\ (p-c(p-1))n + \frac{p(p-1)}{2} \pmod{p(p-1)} & \text{if } n \equiv 0 \pmod{p} \end{cases}$$
where $c$ is any nonzero quadratic residue mod $p$.

These can be verified to work, so we show they are the only functions.

**Step 1.** Take $m \equiv 0$: we obtain $f(0) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$. Substituting $m \to m+p$ into the equation, the LHS is unchanged, but the RHS becomes $f((m+p)n)^n$. Taking $n=1$, we get $f(m) \equiv f(m+p) \pmod{p}$ for all $m$. Since $f$ is surjective mod $p$ and periodic mod $p$ with period $p$, $f$ must be bijective mod $p$.

**Claim 1:** $f(n) \equiv cn \pmod{p}$ for all $n$.

*Proof.* Assume $n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$, so $f(n) \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$. Select $m \equiv \frac{1}{f(n)} \pmod{p}$; then the LHS becomes $1$, so
$$f\!\left(\frac{n}{f(n)}\right)^n \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$
for all such $n$. Comparing the $n$ and $n+p$ cases and applying Fermat's little theorem, we get $f\!\left(\frac{n}{f(n)}\right) \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$. By injectivity, $\frac{n}{f(n)}$ is constant mod $p$, i.e., $f(n) \equiv cn \pmod{p}$. $\square$

**Claim 2:** There exists a function $h$ that is periodic mod $p-1$ with period $p$, such that $f(n) \equiv n + h(n) \pmod{p-1}$ for all $n$.

*Proof.* For $m, n$ nonzero mod $p$:
$$(cmn)^{h(n+(p-1)m)+n+(p-1)m} \equiv (cmn)^n \pmod{p}$$
$$(cmn)^{h(n+(p-1)m)} \equiv 1 \pmod{p}$$
Choosing $m$ so that $cmn$ is a primitive root mod $p$, we get $p-1 \mid h(n+(p-1)m)$. The value $n+(p-1)m$ is surjective mod $p(p-1)$ over $m, n$ nonzero mod $p$, establishing the claimed periodicity. $\square$

From Claim 2, $\operatorname{ord}_p(cmn) \mid h(n-m)$ for all $m, n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$.

**Claim 3:** For any constants $c, t \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$,
$$\operatorname{lcm}_{n}(\operatorname{ord}_p(cn(n+t))) = p-1.$$

*Proof.* Suppose instead the lcm equals some $k < p-1$. Then $c^k x^k(x+t)^k - 1 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$ has a root at each $x \not\equiv 0, -t \pmod{p}$. Therefore $x^{p-1} - 1 \mid c^k x^k(x+t)^{k+1} - (x+t)$ as polynomials in $\mathbb{F}_p[x]$. Hence the reduction of $x^k(x+t)^{k+1}$ modulo $x^{p-1}-1$ has at most 2 nonzero terms. Since $k+1 \le \frac{p+1}{2} < p-1$, the polynomial $(x+t)^{k+1}$ is unchanged by this reduction; but by the Binomial theorem every coefficient $t^{k+1-i}\binom{k+1}{i}$ for $0 \le i \le k+1$ is nonzero in $\mathbb{F}_p$ (since $k+1 < p$), giving more than 2 nonzero terms — contradiction. $\square$

By Claim 3, $h(t) \equiv 0 \pmod{p-1}$ for all $t \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$. Combined with Claim 1, for $n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$: $f(n) \equiv cn \pmod{p}$ and $f(n) \equiv n \pmod{p-1}$, giving $f(n) \equiv (p-c(p-1))n \pmod{p(p-1)}$ by CRT.

**Case $n \equiv 0 \pmod{p}$:** We have $\operatorname{ord}_p(cn^2) \mid h(0)$ for all $n \not\equiv 0 \pmod{p}$. If $p-1 \mid h(0)$, we get the first family.

Otherwise, if $c$ is a quadratic non-residue mod $p$, then $cn^2$ covers all nonzero quadratic non-residues and in particular generators of $\mathbb{F}_p^*$, forcing $p-1 \mid h(0)$, a contradiction.

Therefore $c$ must be a quadratic residue; then $cn^2 = g^2$ for some generator $g$, so $\operatorname{ord}_p(cn^2) = \frac{p-1}{2}$, giving $h(0) \equiv \frac{p-1}{2} \pmod{p-1}$. This yields the second family via CRT. $\blacksquare$

_audit.gap_note: The machinery (Step 1, Claims 1-3) correctly pins down f on inputs n not divisible by p, but the FINAL ANSWER is incomplete: it under-counts the solutions for the n=0 mod p case. The reference treats 'h(0)' as a single value applying to ALL multiples of p simultaneously, concluding either all of them are shifted by (p-1)/2 or none. In fact the FE imposes ord_p(c n^2) | h(x) SEPARATELY for each input residue x = 0 mod p (there are p-1 such residues), so when c is a quadratic residue each multiple of p may INDEPENDENTLY have h(x) in {0,(p-1)/2}. Thus for QR c there are 2^(p-1) admissible functions (any subset of the multiples of p shifted), not just the single 'all shifted' function. Brute force over all m,n for p=11 confirms: shifting only the input class n=11 by 55=p(p-1)/2 (with c=3, a QR) still satisfies the FE and surjectivity; shifting an arbitrary 4-element subset of the multiple-of-p classes also works; for non-QR c=2 any nonzero shift breaks the FE. Secondary (minor) rigor gap: Claim 3's reduction mod x^{p-1}-1 ignores that multiplying (x+t)^{k+1} by x^k shifts exponents up to 2k+1 = p in the boundary case k=(p-1)/2, where wrap-around occurs; the conclusion '>2 nonzero terms' still holds but needs the casework supplied in the fix.

problem:
For every positive integer $n$ with factorization $n = p_1^{\alpha_1} p_2^{\alpha_2} \cdots p_k^{\alpha_k}$ (where $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_k$ are its distinct prime divisors and $\alpha_1, \alpha_2, \ldots, \alpha_k \in \mathbb{N}$) we define
$$f(n) = \alpha_1 \times \alpha_2 \times \cdots \times \alpha_k.$$
We also define $f(n) = f(-n)$ for all $n \in \mathbb{N}$ and $f(1) = f(0) = 1$. Find all polynomials $Q \in \mathbb{Z}[x]$ such that there exist positive integers $N$ and $a > 1$ such that for all integers $n > N$ we have:
$$f(a^n + 1) \mid f(Q(n)).$$
reference_answer: No such polynomial $Q$ exists (the solution set is empty).
solutions:
Claim: no such polynomial Q exists. Suppose, for contradiction, that Q in Z[x], N, a>1 satisfy f(a^n+1) | f(Q(n)) for all n>N. Write Q(x)=x^d L(x) with d>=0 maximal, so L(0)!=0. Set R(x)=Q(gx) below; D=deg Q=deg R.

Step 0 (a large odd prime divisor). The numbers a^m+1 (m>=1) have infinitely many distinct prime divisors (otherwise their bounded radical could not divide unboundedly growing values). Fix an odd prime p with p>|L(0)|+d, p|a^g+1 for some g>=1, and (choosing g minimal) p does not divide g. Let b=v_p(a^g+1)>=1.

Step 1 (LTE). For p odd with p|a^g+1 and any t>=1, since p^t is odd, LTE gives
  v_p(a^{g p^t}+1) = v_p((a^g)^{p^t}+1) = v_p(a^g+1) + v_p(p^t) = b + t.
Enumerate the primes exceeding a large threshold T (with T>b, T>d, T>|L(0)|) as q_1<q_2<... and put t_i = q_i - b (>=1 for q_i>b), n_i = g p^{t_i}. Then v_p(a^{n_i}+1) = q_i, so the exponent q_i appears in the factorization of a^{n_i}+1, whence q_i | f(a^{n_i}+1).

Step 2 (divisibility transfer). Since q_i | f(a^{n_i}+1) | f(Q(n_i)) and f(Q(n_i)) = prod over primes r of v_r(Q(n_i)), the prime q_i divides one factor: there is a prime r_i with q_i | v_{r_i}(Q(n_i)), so v_{r_i}(Q(n_i)) >= q_i, i.e.
  r_i^{q_i} | Q(n_i) = R(p^{t_i}).
(If Q(n_i)=0 then f(Q(n_i))=f(0)=1 and q_i|1 is false; so Q(n_i)!=0 for all large i, and r_i is well defined.)

Step 3 (Claim: the set {r_i} is finite). From r_i^{t_i} <= r_i^{q_i} <= |R(p^{t_i})| <= C p^{D t_i} (C an absolute constant from the leading-coefficient bound on R), we get r_i <= C^{1/t_i} p^D. Since t_i -> infinity, for all sufficiently large i we have r_i <= 2 p^D. Thus all but finitely many r_i lie in the finite set of primes <= 2 p^D; discard the finitely many exceptions. Call the finite set of occurring primes {p_1,...,p_k}.

Step 4 (Claim: r_i != p, corrected). Suppose r_i = p. Because L(0)!=0 and p>|L(0)|, we have L(g p^{t_i}) == L(0) !== 0 (mod p), so v_p(L(g p^{t_i}))=0. Hence
  v_p(Q(n_i)) = v_p((g p^{t_i})^d L(g p^{t_i})) = d*v_p(g) + d*t_i.
If r_i=p then q_i | v_p(Q(n_i)) = d(v_p(g)+t_i). But q_i is prime with q_i>T>d, so q_i does not divide d; and v_p(g)+t_i = v_p(g)+q_i-b == v_p(g)-b (mod q_i), which is a fixed nonzero residue for all q_i larger than |v_p(g)-b|. Hence q_i does not divide d(v_p(g)+t_i) for large q_i, a contradiction. Therefore r_i != p for all (large) i, i.e. p is not among p_1,...,p_k.

Step 5 (forcing a fixed integer root via Dirichlet + Euler). Fix any prime p' > max(p_1,...,p_k,T,p). For each integer alpha>=1 set
  M_alpha = (p_1 p_2 ... p_k)^{alpha-1} * (p_1-1)(p_2-1)...(p_k-1).
Since p' is a prime exceeding every p_ell, gcd(p',M_alpha)=1, so by Dirichlet there is a prime q in our list with q == p' (mod M_alpha) and q-b > alpha; pick such q, let j=j_alpha be its index, t_j=q-b. Its prime r_j lies in {p_1,...,p_k} (Step 3) and r_j != p (Step 4).

Now phi(r_j^alpha) = r_j^{alpha-1}(r_j-1) divides M_alpha, and t_j = q-b == p'-b (mod M_alpha), so t_j == p'-b (mod phi(r_j^alpha)). Since gcd(p,r_j)=1, Euler's theorem gives p^{t_j} == p^{p'-b} (mod r_j^alpha), hence R(p^{t_j}) == R(p^{p'-b}) (mod r_j^alpha). By Step 2, r_j^{q_j} | R(p^{t_j}) with q_j = b+t_j > alpha, so r_j^alpha | R(p^{t_j}). Therefore
  R(p^{p'-b}) == 0 (mod r_j^alpha).
As alpha -> infinity, r_j^alpha >= 2^alpha -> infinity while R(p^{p'-b}) is a fixed integer; hence R(p^{p'-b}) = 0. Equivalently g p^{p'-b} is a root of Q.

Step 6 (conclusion). There are infinitely many primes p' > max(p_1,...,p_k,T,p), and distinct p' give distinct exponents p'-b, hence distinct roots g p^{p'-b} of Q. A nonzero polynomial has finitely many roots, so Q == 0. But then f(Q(n)) = f(0) = 1 for all n, while f(a^{n_i}+1) >= q_i -> infinity, so f(a^{n_i}+1) | f(Q(n_i))=1 fails. This contradiction shows no Q satisfies the condition. Hence the solution set is empty. QED
_original_solutions:
We claim no such polynomial $Q$ exists.

Since infinitely many primes divide some number of the form $a^n + 1$ (by Zsygmondy), we can find an odd positive integer $r$ such that $q \mid a^r + 1$ for some odd prime $q > X$, for any $X$.

Take $n = r \cdot m q^k$ for positive integers $m, k$ with $m$ odd and not divisible by $q$. By LTE:
$$v_q(a^n + 1) = v_q((a^r)^{mq^k} + 1) = v_q(a^r + 1) + v_q(mq^k) = v_q(a^r + 1) + k.$$
This implies $Q$ cannot be constant, since then $v_q(a^r+1)+k \mid f(Q(n))$ for all $k$.

**Claim:** Fix any positive integer $k$. There exists a constant $C$ (depending only on $r$ and $q$, independent of $k$) such that there exist infinitely many $n$ of the form $r \cdot m q^k$ where $v_p(Q(n)) < C$ for all primes $p \neq q$.

*Proof.* Let $r = r' \cdot q^c$ with $q \nmid r'$, so we need $n = m \cdot r' \cdot q^{k+c}$. For small primes $p \le M$ (to be chosen), fix an odd integer $u$ with $Q(r'u) \neq 0$. Set
$$D = \prod_{\substack{\text{prime } p \le M \\ p \neq q}} p^{v_p(Q(r'u))+1}.$$
By CRT, find $A$ with $A \equiv r'u \pmod{Dr'}$ and $A \equiv q^{k+c} \pmod{q^{k+c+1}}$, and set $B = Dr'q^{k+c+1}$, $n = An' + B$. Then $v_q(n) = k+c$, $n$ is odd, $r' \mid n$, and for primes $p \le M$, $p \neq q$: $v_p(Q(n)) \le v_p(Q(r'u))$.

For primes $p > M$: let $Q = e \cdot Q_1^{k_1} \cdots Q_s^{k_s}$ in $\mathbb{Z}[x]$. By Bézout, for each $i$ there is a constant $y_i$ in the ideal $(Q_i, Q_i')$. Choose $M > r', q$ and $M > |y_i|$ for all $i$; then for $p > M$, $Q_i$ has no double root mod $p$. By Hensel lifting, for each $t$, the density of $n'$ with $p^t \mid Q_i(An'+B)$ for some $i$ is at most $d/p^t$. Taking $t$ large enough so that $1 - d(\zeta(t)-1) > 0$, we find infinitely many $n'$ with $v_p(Q(n)) < tK + v_p(e)$ for all $p > M$. This $C$ is independent of $k$. $\square$

Now pick $k$ so that $v_q(a^r+1) + k = P$ is a prime greater than $C$. There exist infinitely many $n = r \cdot mq^k$ with $v_p(Q(n)) < C < P$ for all $p \neq q$. Since $P \mid f(a^n+1)$, we need $P \mid f(Q(n))$, which forces $P \mid v_q(Q(n))$.

Write $Q(x) = x^b R(x)$ with $R(0) \neq 0$. Choose $q > |R(0)|$; then $q \nmid R(n)$, so $v_q(Q(n)) = b(k + v_q(r))$. Hence $P \mid b(k + v_q(r))$, and since $P = v_q(a^r+1) + k$, subtracting gives $P \mid b(v_q(r) - v_q(a^r+1))$, so eventually $v_q(r) = v_q(a^r+1)$ for large primes $P$.

This shows $q \mid a^r + 1 \Rightarrow q \mid r$ for any odd positive integer $r$. But by Fermat, $q \mid r + q - 1$, so $q \mid 1$, a contradiction. $\blacksquare$

We claim no such polynomial $Q$ exists. Assume the contrary. Define $L(x)$ so that $Q(x) = x^d L(x)$ where $d$ is maximal, so $L(0) \neq 0$.

Fix an odd prime $p > |L(0)| + d$ dividing $a^g + 1$ for some positive integer $g$, and let $\nu_p(a^g+1) = b$ with $p \nmid b$ (replacing $g$ with $gp$ if needed).

Enumerate all primes larger than some threshold $T$ (say $T > (Nb)!$) as $q_1, q_2, \ldots$, and let $t_i = q_i - b$. By LTE:
$$\nu_p(a^{g \cdot p^{t_i}} + 1) = b + t_i = q_i.$$

Let $R(x) = Q(gx)$. Then $q_i \mid f(a^{g \cdot p^{t_i}}+1) \mid f(R(p^{t_i}))$, so for each $i$ there is a prime $r_i$ with $q_i \mid \nu_{r_i}(R(p^{t_i}))$, giving $r_i^{t_i} \mid R(p^{t_i})$.

**Claim 1.** $r_i \neq p$ for any $i$.

*Proof.* If $r_i = p$ then $p \mid \nu_p(R(p^{t_i}))$. But $\nu_p(R(p^{t_i})) = d \cdot t_i + \nu_p(L(gp^{t_i})) = d(p-b)$ (since $p \nmid L(gp^{t_i})$ by choice of $p$), and $p \nmid d$ or $p - b$ by choice, contradiction. $\square$

**Claim 2.** The set $\{r_i\}$ is finite.

*Proof.* If not, some $r_j > p^{c \deg R}$ for arbitrarily large $c$, giving $R(p^{t_j}) \ge r_j^{t_j} \ge (p^{t_j})^{c \deg R}$, contradicting the degree bound on $R$. $\square$

So all $r_i$ lie in a finite set of primes $\{p_1, \ldots, p_k\}$, none equal to $p$. WLOG each $p_\ell$ occurs as $r_i$ infinitely often.

Fix a prime $p' > \max(p_1, \ldots, p_k, T, p)$ and arbitrarily large $\alpha$. By Dirichlet, choose $j_\alpha$ so that $q_{j_\alpha} \equiv p' \pmod{(p_1 \cdots p_k)^{\alpha-1}(p_1-1)\cdots(p_k-1)}$ and $t_{j_\alpha} > \alpha$. Then by Euler's theorem:
$$R(p^{p'-b}) \equiv R(p^{t_{j_\alpha}}) \equiv 0 \pmod{p_{c_{j_\alpha}}^\alpha}.$$
As $\alpha \to \infty$, $R(p^{p'-b})$ (a fixed constant) is divisible by arbitrarily large prime powers, forcing $R(p^{p'-b}) = 0$, i.e., $p^{p'-b}$ is a root of $R$, so $gp^{p'-b}$ is a root of $Q$.

Since Zsygmondy guarantees infinitely many primes dividing $a^n + 1$ as $n$ varies, the same argument gives infinitely many distinct roots of $Q$, forcing $Q \equiv 0$. But then $f(Q(n)) = 1$ for all $n$, which fails the divisibility condition. Contradiction. $\blacksquare$

_audit.gap_note: Solution 2's strategy (engineer n=g*p^t so v_p(a^n+1)=q a large prime forcing q | f(Q(n)), deduce r^q | Q(n), bound the prime r to a finite set, then use Dirichlet+Euler to pin down an integer root p^{p'-b} of R, yielding infinitely many roots and Q==0) is fully correct and complete. The only real defect is the write-up of Claim 1 (r_i != p): it states v_p(R(p^{t_i})) = d(p-b) and argues 'p does not divide d or p-b', which is garbled/incorrect. The correct argument: v_p(Q(g p^{t_i})) = d*v_p(g) + d*t_i (since p does not divide L(0), so v_p(L)=0); if r_i=p then q_i | d(v_p(g)+t_i), but q_i does not divide d and v_p(g)+t_i == v_p(g)-b (mod q_i) is nonzero for large q_i, contradiction. The claim is true; only its justification needs repair. The remark 'with p not dividing b (replacing g by gp)' is unnecessary and is not used (LTE v_p(a^{gp^t}+1)=b+t needs only p odd, p|a^g+1). All other steps (LTE, divisibility transfer q_i | v_{r_i}(Q(n)), the size/degree bound giving finiteness of {r_i}, and the Dirichlet/Euler congruence p^{t_j}==p^{p'-b} mod r_j^alpha using phi(r_j^alpha)|M_alpha and gcd(p,r_j)=1) are correct. Answer 'no such Q' is correct.

problem:
A sequence $\{a_n\}$ $(n \ge 1)$ satisfies

$$a_1 = a_2 = 1$$

and for all positive integers $n$,

$$a_{n+2} = a_n + \frac{1}{a_{n+1}^2 + a_n^2}.$$

Determine whether there exist infinitely many positive integers $n$ such that

$$a_{2n}^3 > \frac{3n}{2} - 2026\sqrt{n}.$$
reference_answer: No, there do not exist infinitely many such $n$; in fact $a_{2n}^3 < \frac{3n}{2} - \frac{3}{200}n^{2/3}$ for all sufficiently large $n$, so the inequality $a_{2n}^3 > \frac{3n}{2} - 2026\sqrt{n}$ holds for only finitely many $n$.
solutions:
ANSWER: No. The inequality a_{2n}^3 > 3n/2 - 2026*sqrt(n) holds for only finitely many n.

Notation. Write r_k = 1/(a_{k+1}^2 + a_k^2), so the recursion is a_{k+2} = a_k + r_k.

Step 0 (positivity, monotonicity, lower bound). a_1=a_2=1>0, and if a_k,a_{k+1}>0 then a_{k+2}=a_k+r_k>0; so a_n>0 for all n. Also a_{k+2}-a_k=r_k>0, so each of the even- and odd-indexed subsequences is strictly increasing; hence a_{2k}>=a_2=1 and a_{2k-1}>=a_1=1, i.e. a_n>=1 for all n. Consequently a_{k+2}>a_k gives a_{k+2}^2+a_{k+1}^2 > a_{k+1}^2+a_k^2, so r_{k+1}<r_k: the sequence (r_k) is strictly decreasing.

Step 1 (odd terms are strict local maxima; gap bounds). Put g_n=a_{2n+1}-a_{2n} and h_n=a_{2n+1}-a_{2n+2}. From the recursion h_n = a_{2n+1}-(a_{2n}+r_{2n}) = g_n - r_{2n}, and
  g_{n+1} = a_{2n+3}-a_{2n+2} = (a_{2n+1}+r_{2n+1})-(a_{2n}+r_{2n}) = g_n - (r_{2n}-r_{2n+1}).
Since (r_k) is decreasing, r_{2n}-r_{2n+1}>0, so g_{n+1}<g_n; with g_1=a_3-a_2=1/2 this gives 0<... and in particular g_n<=g_1=1/2 for all n.  (We do not yet know g_n>0; we prove it now.)
Telescoping and using that (r_k) is decreasing,
  sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(r_{2k}-r_{2k+1}) <= sum_{j=2}^{2n-2}(r_j-r_{j+1}) = r_2 - r_{2n-1} < r_2,
because the pairs (r_{2k},r_{2k+1}) are disjoint sub-intervals of the decreasing chain r_2>r_3>... and the omitted gaps r_{2k+1}-r_{2k+2}>=0. Hence
  g_n = g_1 - sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(r_{2k}-r_{2k+1}) > g_1 - r_2 = 1/2 - 1/(a_3^2+a_2^2) = 1/2 - 1/(9/4+1) = 1/2 - 4/13 = 5/26 > 0.
Thus a_{2n+1}>a_{2n} for all n. For h_n: h_n = g_n - r_{2n} = g_1 - [ sum_{k=1}^{n-1}(r_{2k}-r_{2k+1}) + r_{2n} ]. The bracket is (r_2-r_3)+(r_4-r_5)+...+(r_{2n-2}-r_{2n-1}) + r_{2n} <= (r_2 - r_{2n-1}) + r_{2n} < r_2 (since r_{2n}<r_{2n-1}); for n=1 the bracket equals r_2 exactly. Therefore h_n >= g_1 - r_2 = 5/26 > 0 for all n>=1 (equality at n=1). In particular a_{2n+1}>a_{2n+2}, and h_n >= 5/26.

Step 2 (upper bound a_{2n}^3 < 3n/2). Fix n and write x=a_{2n}, y=a_{2n+1}, z=a_{2n+2}, so x,z<y (Step 1) and z>x (Step 0). Factoring,
  a_{2n+2}^3 - a_{2n}^3 = (z-x)(z^2+zx+x^2) = r_{2n}(z^2+zx+x^2) = (z^2+zx+x^2)/(y^2+x^2).
We claim z^2+zx+x^2 < (3/2)(y^2+x^2), i.e. z^2+zx - x^2/2 < (3/2)y^2. Since 0<z<y and x>0 we have z^2<y^2 and zx<yx, so
  z^2+zx - x^2/2 < y^2+yx - x^2/2 = (3/2)y^2 - (1/2)(y-x)^2 <= (3/2)y^2,
the identity (3/2)y^2-(y^2+yx-x^2/2) = (1/2)(y-x)^2>=0 being the last step. Hence a_{2n+2}^3 - a_{2n}^3 < 3/2. Telescoping from a_2=1,
  a_{2n}^3 < a_2^3 + (3/2)(n-1) = 3n/2 - 1/2 < 3n/2.
